# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]
**"List the major components of the hydrological cycle and briefly explain the role of infiltration in the cycle." (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The major components of the hydrological cycle are: (i) **Precipitation (P)**, (ii) **Evaporation (E)**, (iii) **Transpiration (T)**, (iv) **Evapotranspiration (ET)**, (v) **Condensation**, (vi) **Infiltration (f)**, (vii) **Percolation**, (viii) **Runoff (R)**, (ix) **Sublimation**, and (x) **Storage** (atmospheric, surface, soil moisture, groundwater).

**Role of infiltration:** Infiltration is the process by which surface water enters the soil. It partitions incoming precipitation into (a) deep percolation that recharges groundwater and ultimately contributes to baseflow, and (b) soil moisture that supports plant transpiration. The portion that does *not* infiltrate becomes surface runoff. Infiltration is therefore a critical control on both the *magnitude* and *timing* of runoff, and on groundwater recharge. Horton's equation,

$$
f(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c) e^{-kt}
$$

quantifies the time-decay of infiltration capacity.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]
**"Define a watershed. List any four physical characteristics used to describe a watershed." (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A **watershed** (also called a *catchment*, *drainage basin*, or *river basin*) is a topographically bounded land area, enclosed by a drainage divide, in which all precipitation that falls ultimately drains past a single common outlet.

Four physical characteristics of a watershed are:
1. **Watershed area (A)** — usually expressed in km².
2. **Drainage density ($D_d$)** — total stream length per unit area.
3. **Form factor ($F_f = A/L^2$)** — describes basin shape.
4. **Relief ratio ($R_r = H/L$)** — describes basin steepness.

Other acceptable answers: stream frequency, compactness ratio, circularity ratio, elongation ratio, average slope, stream order.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Alternative 1 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, ESE Pattern]**
**(a)** Derive the water balance equation for a watershed, clearly stating the assumptions. Apply it to estimate the mean annual runoff from a watershed given $P = 2200$ mm and $ET = 1250$ mm, assuming negligible change in storage. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the following watershed characteristics with formulas and physical significance: (i) Drainage density, (ii) Stream frequency, (iii) Form factor, (iv) Compactness ratio, (v) Relief ratio. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Statement of continuity (1 mark)**
For any control volume, conservation of mass requires:

$$
\text{Inflow} - \text{Outflow} = \Delta S
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the control volume = watershed and list fluxes (2 marks)**
- Inflow: $P$ (precipitation), $G_{in}$ (subsurface inflow from neighbouring basin)
- Outflow: $R$ (surface runoff at outlet), $ET$ (evapotranspiration), $G_{out}$ (subsurface outflow)
- Storage: $\Delta S$ (change in soil moisture + groundwater + surface storage)

**Step 3 — Write the general water balance (1 mark)**

$$
P + G_{in} = R + ET + G_{out} + \Delta S
$$

**Step 4 — Assumptions for the simplified form (1 mark)**
1. The watershed is *isolated*: $G_{in} = 0$ and $G_{out} = 0$.
2. The period is *long-term* (multi-year average): $\Delta S \to 0$.

**Step 5 — Simplified form (1 mark)**

$$
P = R + ET
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical application (1 mark)**

$$
R = P - ET = 2200 - 1250 = 950 \text{ mm}
$$

Therefore, the **mean annual runoff depth = 950 mm**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

(i) **Drainage density**

$$
D_d = \frac{\Sigma L_i}{A}
$$

where $\Sigma L_i$ = total length of all streams (km), $A$ = watershed area (km²). Units: km/km². *Physical significance:* Indicates the closeness of stream channels. High $D_d$ → fine drainage network → impermeable surface or steep terrain → flashier runoff response.

(ii) **Stream frequency**

$$
F_s = \frac{N}{A}
$$

where $N$ = total number of streams of all orders. Units: number/km². *Physical significance:* Indicates stream population density; reflects lithology, vegetation, and rainfall.

(iii) **Form factor (Horton)**

$$
F_f = \frac{A}{L^2}
$$

where $L$ = length of the main channel. *Physical significance:* $F_f = 0$ for a very elongated basin; $F_f \to 0.79$ for a perfectly circular basin. Low $F_f$ → longer time of concentration → lower, more spread-out flood peak.

(iv) **Compactness ratio (Gravelius)**

$$
C_c = \frac{0.2821 \, P_b}{\sqrt{A}}
$$

where $P_b$ = perimeter of the basin. *Physical significance:* $C_c = 1$ for a perfect circle; $C_c > 1$ indicates deviation from circularity, i.e., elongation. A high $C_c$ watershed produces runoff more slowly than a circular one of equal area.

(v) **Relief ratio**

$$
R_r = \frac{H}{L}
$$

where $H$ = maximum − minimum elevation, $L$ = main channel length. *Physical significance:* Indicates overall steepness; controls the *velocity* of overland flow and the *kinetic energy* available for soil erosion.

---

### Question B — Alternative 2 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, ESE Pattern]**
**(a)** What is infiltration? Derive the Horton cumulative infiltration equation starting from the infiltration capacity equation. Define every term. (7 marks)

**(b)** A small agricultural watershed in Palakkad, Kerala, has the following properties:
Area $A = 18 \text{ km}^2$, total stream length $\Sigma L_i = 30 \text{ km}$, number of streams $N = 12$, main stream length $L = 7 \text{ km}$, perimeter $P_b = 22 \text{ km}$, total relief $H = 180 \text{ m}$, annual rainfall $P = 1800$ mm, and annual evapotranspiration $ET = 950$ mm. Compute the drainage density, stream frequency, form factor, compactness ratio, relief ratio, and mean annual runoff depth. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Definition of infiltration (2 marks)**
**Infiltration** is the downward movement of water from the surface into the soil. It is controlled by soil texture, structure, initial moisture, vegetative cover, and rainfall intensity. The *infiltration capacity* $f$ is the maximum rate at which soil can absorb water.

**Step 2 — Horton's infiltration capacity equation (1 mark)**

$$
f(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c) e^{-kt}
$$

where
- $f(t)$ = infiltration capacity at time $t$ (mm/h)
- $f_0$ = initial infiltration capacity (mm/h)
- $f_c$ = final (equilibrium) infiltration capacity (mm/h)
- $k$ = Horton's decay constant (h⁻¹)
- $t$ = time elapsed since start of ponding (h)

**Step 3 — Cumulative infiltration defined (1 mark)**

$$
F(t) = \int_0^t f(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and split the integral (1 mark)**

$$
F(t) = \int_0^t f_c \, d\tau + (f_0 - f_c) \int_0^t e^{-k\tau} \, d\tau
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate (1 mark)**

$$
F(t) = f_c \, t + \frac{(f_0 - f_c)}{k} \left(1 - e^{-kt}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Limiting behaviour note (1 mark)**
As $t \to \infty$, $F(t) \to f_c \, t$, confirming the model is physically consistent.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

(i) **Drainage density**

$$
D_d = \frac{30}{18} = 1.667 \text{ km/km}^2
$$

(ii) **Stream frequency**

$$
F_s = \frac{12}{18} = 0.667 \text{ streams/km}^2
$$

(iii) **Form factor**

$$
F_f = \frac{18}{7^2} = \frac{18}{49} = 0.367
$$

(iv) **Compactness ratio**

$$
C_c = \frac{0.2821 \times 22}{\sqrt{18}} = \frac{6.206}{4.243} = 1.463
$$

(v) **Relief ratio**

$$
R_r = \frac{180}{7000} = 0.0257
$$

(vi) **Mean annual runoff depth (using $P = R + ET$)**

$$
R = 1800 - 950 = 850 \text{ mm}
$$

**Summary of computed values:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $D_d$ | 1.667 km/km² |
| $F_s$ | 0.667 /km² |
| $F_f$ | 0.367 |
| $C_c$ | 1.463 |
| $R_r$ | 0.0257 |
| $R$ | 850 mm |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit consistency** — Drainage density is *always* in km/km²; do not write km² or km⁻¹.
> 2. **Length unit** — When using $H/L$ in relief ratio, both must be the *same* unit (convert m to km or vice versa). Mixing units is the #1 cause of mark loss.
> 3. **Sign convention in water balance** — $\Delta S$ can be positive (wet year, storage builds) or negative (dry year). Students often omit it entirely; KTU examiners *expect* the full equation first, then the simplification.
> 4. **Horton units** — $k$ is in **h⁻¹ or min⁻¹**, not dimensionless. Time $t$ must use the *same* unit as $1/k$.
> 5. **Form factor** — $F_f$ for an *elongated* basin is always < 0.5. Stating "$F_f$ = 0.367, so basin is elongated" is a full-credit conclusion.
> 6. **Compactness ratio** — $C_c \geq 1$ *always*; only a perfect circle gives $C_c = 1$. Stating "$C_c$ = 1.463, so basin is more elongated than circular" earns the interpretation mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final high-density checklist — re-read this on the morning of the exam:**

- [ ] The **hydrological cycle** has **ten standard components**: P, E, T, ET, condensation, infiltration, percolation, runoff, sublimation, storage.
- [ ] The **water balance equation** is the conservation-of-mass identity:

$$
P = R + ET + \Delta S + G_{out} - G_{in}
$$

- [ ] The **long-term average** simplifies to $P = R + ET$ (this is the most-quoted form).
- [ ] **Horton's infiltration capacity** is an *exponentially decaying* function from $f_0$ to $f_c$ with decay constant $k$.
- [ ] **Horton's cumulative infiltration** is its time-integral — must derive from the integral, not memorize both.
- [ ] **Watershed area** units: 1 km² = 100 ha = 10⁶ m².
- [ ] **Drainage density** $D_d = \Sigma L_i / A$ (km/km²) — high $D_d$ → flashy basin.
- [ ] **Stream frequency** $F_s = N / A$ — reflects lithology and rainfall.
- [ ] **Form factor** $F_f = A / L^2$ — low value means elongated basin, longer lag.
- [ ] **Compactness ratio** $C_c = 0.2821 P_b / \sqrt{A}$ — $C_c = 1$ for a circle; always $\geq 1$.
- [ ] **Relief ratio** $R_r = H / L$ — keep $H$ and $L$ in *same units* (commonly convert both to km).
- [ ] **Strahler stream order** — order increases *only* when two streams of the *same* order join.
- [ ] **Rational method** $Q_p = 0.00278 \, C \, I \, A$ ($Q_p$ in m³/s, $I$ in mm/h, $A$ in ha) — used for *small* urban / agricultural catchments (< 50 km²).
- [ ] **Penman-Monteith** is the FAO-recommended $ET_o$ method; **Hargreaves** is a temperature-only fallback.
- [ ] **Evapotranspiration > Evaporation** *always* (since $ET = E + T \geq E$).
- [ ] In **Kerala's lateritic / tropical setting**, expect moderate-to-high $D_d$ (≈ 1–3 km/km²), high annual rainfall (≈ 2000–3500 mm), and runoff coefficients of 0.4–0.6 for forested catchments.
- [ ] **Examiner's two-second grading cue:** Always state *units* in your final answer. A correctly computed numerical value with no unit gets *partial* credit only.
- [ ] **Memory trick for the morphometric formulas:** "**A**rea **L**ength **P**erimeter **H**eight" — $A/L^2$, $A/P_b^2$, $H/L$. Every morphometric parameter is just a *ratio* of these four building blocks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Watershed Characteristics (Size, Shape, Slope, Drainage Pattern)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET723 - WATERSHED CONSERVATION AND MANGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Watershed Characteristics (Size, Shape, Slope, Drainage Pattern)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Watershed?

A **watershed** (also called a *drainage basin* or *catchment*) is a geographically delineated land area, bounded by a *drainage divide* (ridge line), in which all precipitation that falls will eventually drain to a common outlet point such as a river, lake, reservoir, or estuary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** "A watershed is a topographically enclosed area that collects, stores, and discharges precipitation runoff through a single common outlet, governed by gravity, surface morphology, and subsurface geology."

The boundaries of a watershed are called the **watershed divide** or **ridgeline**. A drop of rain that falls *anywhere* inside this boundary will, by gravitational flow, exit through the single principal outlet. Anything falling *outside* the divide drains to a different watershed entirely.

## 1.2 What are Watershed Characteristics?

**Watershed characteristics** are the *quantitative and qualitative descriptors* of the physical, hydrological, and morphological attributes of a drainage basin. They govern how the basin responds to rainfall (i.e., the **runoff hydrograph**, peak flow, base flow, and sediment yield).

These characteristics are classified into three categories:

| Category | Examples | Influence on Hydrology |
|----------|----------|------------------------|
| **Morphometric / Geometric** | Size, Shape, Slope, Drainage Pattern | Runoff concentration, time of travel |
| **Drainage Network** | Stream order, drainage density, bifurcation ratio | Drainage efficiency, flood peaks |
| **Relief / Topographic** | Relief, relief ratio, ruggedness number | Erosion, sediment yield |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Funnel of a Bathtub" 🍯

Imagine a **bathtub** lying in your garden, tipped slightly so that water drains out from one corner (the outlet). The **inside walls of the bathtub** are the *watershed boundaries (divides)*. Any water falling *inside* the tub flows to the drain — that is the watershed. Water falling *outside* the tub simply misses it entirely.

- **Size** = The capacity of the tub (how much water it can collect).
- **Shape** = Whether the tub is round, square, elongated, or pear-shaped (affects how fast water concentrates at the drain).
- **Slope** = How steeply the tub is tilted (steeper = faster draining).
- **Drainage pattern** = The little channels and grooves inside the tub that direct water to the drain (like veins on a leaf).

## 1.4 Why are Watershed Characteristics Critical?

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Focus:** Almost every question in this module is based on numerical evaluation of these characteristics. They form the foundation for hydrological modeling, reservoir design, flood estimation, and erosion control planning.

The standard physical metrics used throughout this module are:

- **Area (A)** — measured in **km²** (for large basins) or **hectares** (for small ones).
- **Perimeter (P)** — measured in **km**.
- **Length of the basin (L_b)** — measured in **km** (longest dimension).
- **Stream order** — a dimensionless integer (1, 2, 3, ...).
- **Relief (H)** — measured in **m** (difference between highest and lowest point).
- **Drainage density (D_d)** — measured in **km/km²**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component 1 — Size of the Watershed

### 2.1.1 Theory
The **size** of a watershed is simply its aerial extent (catchment area, A). It is the single most fundamental parameter because:

- It directly controls the **volume of runoff** generated.
- It is used as the denominator in almost every *normalized* parameter (drainage density, stream frequency, form factor, etc.).
- Larger watersheds tend to have **lower peak discharges per unit area** because of attenuation and channel storage.

### 2.1.2 Classification of Watersheds by Size

| Class | Area Range | Typical Examples |
|-------|-----------|------------------|
| **Micro-watershed** | < 1 km² (100 ha) | Farm ponds, micro check dams |
| **Mini-watershed** | 1 – 10 km² | Village-level planning |
| **Small watershed** | 10 – 100 km² | Sub-watershed development |
| **Medium watershed** | 100 – 1000 km² | Block / taluk level |
| **Large watershed** | > 1000 km² | River basin |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** "Smaller watersheds respond faster (shorter time of concentration) but produce lower total volume. Larger watersheds produce higher total volume but with delayed, attenuated peaks."

### 2.1.3 Methods to Measure Watershed Area
1. **Planimeter method** — mechanical/electronic planimeter on a topographic map.
2. **Grid method** — count grid squares falling within the boundary.
3. **GIS/Digital method** — polygon area in ArcGIS/QGIS using shapefile of the divide.
4. **Surveying** — traverse method for very small watersheds.

---

## 2.2 Component 2 — Shape of the Watershed

### 2.2.1 Theory
The **shape** governs the **rate of runoff concentration** at the outlet. A *pear-shaped* (elongated) basin delivers water slowly; a *fan-shaped* (compact) basin delivers water rapidly, producing sharper, higher flood peaks.

### 2.2.2 Shape Parameters (Quantitative Descriptors)

The four key shape parameters, all of which are **high-frequency KTU exam topics**, are:

**(a) Form Factor (R_f)** — Horton (1932)

$$R_f = \frac{A}{L_b^{\,2}}$$

where $A$ = basin area, $L_b$ = basin length. For a perfectly circular basin, $R_f = \pi/4 \approx 0.785$. Values of $R_f$ approaching 1.0 indicate a near-circular, hydrologically responsive basin; values < 0.3 indicate elongated basins with delayed runoff.

**(b) Compactness Coefficient (C_c)** — Gravelius (1914)

$$C_c = 0.2821 \cdot \frac{P}{\sqrt{A}} \;\; \text{(or equivalently)} \;\; C_c = \frac{P}{P_c}$$

where $P$ = actual basin perimeter, $P_c$ = perimeter of a circle of equal area, $0.2821 = 1/\sqrt{\pi}$. Always $C_c \geq 1$. A perfect circle gives $C_c = 1$.

**(c) Circularity Ratio (R_c)** — Miller (1953)

$$R_c = \frac{4\pi A}{P^{2}} = \frac{1}{C_c^{\,2}}$$

Range: $0 < R_c \leq 1$. Equals 1 for a perfect circle. Inversely related to $C_c$.

**(d) Elongation Ratio (R_e)** — Schumm (1956)

$$R_e = \frac{2 \sqrt{A / \pi}}{L_b} = \frac{D_c}{L_b}$$

where $D_c$ = diameter of a circle with the same area as the basin. $R_e$ ranges from 0 (highly elongated line) to 1 (perfect circle). $R_e > 0.9$ indicates circular, $0.6$–$0.9$ oval, $< 0.6$ elongated.

### 2.2.3 Physical Meaning

> [!NOTE]
> **High-yield:** A basin with high $R_f$, $R_c$, $R_e$ and low $C_c$ (closer to 1) is *compact*, generating **higher peak flows** and **shorter time of concentration**. Elongated basins produce **flatter, delayed hydrographs**.

---

## 2.3 Component 3 — Slope of the Watershed

### 2.3.1 Theory
Slope controls the **velocity of overland flow**, **infiltration time**, **erosion potential**, and **time of concentration**. Steep slopes = fast runoff = high flood peaks, high erosion, low infiltration.

### 2.3.2 Types of Slope
1. **Land slope (overland slope)** — slope of the ground surface.
2. **Channel slope** — slope of the main stream.
3. **Average basin slope** — overall slope used in hydrology models.
4. **Relief (H)** — maximum vertical drop in the basin.

### 2.3.3 Methods to Compute Average Basin Slope

**(a) Horton's Method (Mean Slope)**

$$\bar{S} = \frac{\Delta H \cdot \Sigma L}{A}$$

where $\Delta H$ = contour interval, $\Sigma L$ = total length of all contours inside the basin, $A$ = basin area.

**(b) Area-Weighted Method**

$$\bar{S} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i \cdot A_i}{\sum A_i}$$

where $S_i$ = slope of the $i$-th strip between two consecutive contours, $A_i$ = area of that strip.

**(c) Wentworth's Method**

$$\bar{S} = \frac{N \cdot \Delta H \cdot l}{\Sigma L}$$

where $N$ = number of contour intersections with a grid of $N$ horizontal lines, $l$ = length of each grid line.

### 2.3.4 Relief Parameters

- **Relief (H)** = Maximum elevation − Minimum elevation (in m).
- **Relief Ratio (R_h)** = H / L_b (dimensionless, m/km).
- **Ruggedness Number (R_n)** = H × D_d (dimensionless).

---

## 2.4 Component 4 — Drainage Pattern

### 2.4.1 Theory
A **drainage pattern** is the spatial arrangement of streams in a watershed as seen on a map. It reflects the underlying **geology, structure, and slope** of the terrain.

### 2.4.2 Stream Order Systems

**Horton's Stream Order (Modified by Strahler):**
- **1st order** — smallest fingertip streams with no tributaries.
- **2nd order** — formed by junction of two 1st-order streams.
- **3rd order** — formed by junction of two 2nd-order streams, etc.
- A higher-order stream is formed only when **two streams of the same order** join. If a lower-order meets a higher-order, the order of the receiving stream does not change.

### 2.4.3 Quantitative Drainage Network Parameters

| Parameter | Formula | Definition |
|-----------|---------|-----------|
| **Drainage Density (D_d)** | $D_d = \Sigma L_u / A$ | Total stream length per unit area; high D_d = impervious, fine-textured, erodible terrain |
| **Stream Frequency (F_s)** | $F_s = N_u / A$ | Number of streams per unit area; indicates dissection |
| **Bifurcation Ratio (R_b)** | $R_b = N_u / N_{u+1}$ | Ratio of streams of order $u$ to order $u+1$; typical 3–5 |
| **Length Ratio (R_L)** | $R_L = \bar{L}_{u+1} / \bar{L}_u$ | Ratio of mean stream lengths of consecutive orders |
| **Length of Overland Flow (L_g)** | $L_g = 1 / (2 \cdot D_d)$ | Half the reciprocal of drainage density |
| **Constant of Channel Maintenance (C)** | $C = 1 / D_d$ | Area required to sustain one unit length of stream |

### 2.4.4 Types of Drainage Patterns

| Pattern | Description | Indicates |
|---------|-------------|-----------|
| **Dendritic** | Tree-like, irregular branching | Homogeneous, uniform lithology |
| **Trellis** | Rectangular, parallel main streams with right-angle tributaries | Folded sedimentary rocks, ridges & valleys |
| **Rectangular** | Right-angle bends | Jointed/faulted rocks |
| **Radial** | Streams flow outward from a central high | Volcano, dome |
| **Annular** | Concentric ring pattern | Eroded dome with alternating hard/soft rocks |
| **Parallel** | Streams parallel to each other | Moderate-to-steep uniform slope |
| **Centripetal** | Streams converge to a central low | Crater, basin |
| **Deranged** | No definite pattern, swamps, lakes | Glaciated terrain |

### 2.4.5 Horton's Laws (Foundational Theorems)

1. **Law of Stream Numbers:** $N_u = R_b^{\,(k - u)}$
2. **Law of Stream Lengths:** $\bar{L}_u = \bar{L}_1 \cdot R_L^{\,(u - 1)}$

These two power laws together describe the geometric self-similarity of natural drainage networks.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The table below is a complete reference of all formulas you need for any numerical problem in this topic. Memorize the formulas and the units in **bold**.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Range / Typical |
|---|-----------|---------|------|----------------|
| 1 | **Form Factor (R_f)** | $R_f = A / L_b^{\,2}$ | dimensionless | 0 to 0.785 (circular) |
| 2 | **Compactness Coefficient (C_c)** | $C_c = 0.2821 \cdot P / \sqrt{A}$ | dimensionless | $\geq 1$ |
| 3 | **Circularity Ratio (R_c)** | $R_c = 4\pi A / P^{2}$ | dimensionless | 0 to 1 |
| 4 | **Elongation Ratio (R_e)** | $R_e = 2 \sqrt{A/\pi} \,/\, L_b$ | dimensionless | 0 to 1 |
| 5 | **Drainage Density (D_d)** | $D_d = \Sigma L / A$ | **km/km²** | 0.5 – 5.0 |
| 6 | **Stream Frequency (F_s)** | $F_s = N / A$ | **1/km²** | 0.5 – 5.0 |
| 7 | **Bifurcation Ratio (R_b)** | $R_b = N_u / N_{u+1}$ | dimensionless | 2 – 5 |
| 8 | **Relief Ratio (R_h)** | $R_h = H / L_b$ | **m/km** | < 0.1 low, > 0.5 high |
| 9 | **Ruggedness Number (R_n)** | $R_n = H \cdot D_d$ | dimensionless | – |
| 10 | **Overland Flow Length (L_g)** | $L_g = 1 / (2 D_d)$ | **km** | – |
| 11 | **Channel Maintenance (C)** | $C = 1 / D_d$ | **km²/km** | – |
| 12 | **Horton: Stream Numbers** | $N_u = R_b^{\,(k-u)}$ | – | – |
| 13 | **Horton: Stream Lengths** | $\bar{L}_u = \bar{L}_1 \cdot R_L^{\,(u-1)}$ | **km** | – |
| 14 | **Average Slope (Horton)** | $\bar{S} = \Delta H \cdot \Sigma L / A$ | – | – |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban hydrology:** Compact, impervious watersheds need **storm-water detention ponds** to flatten hydrographs.
- **Flood modeling:** Peak flow estimation (Rational method, SCS-CN) requires $A$, $L_b$, $C_c$, $R_h$.
- **Reservoir design:** Catchment yield ≈ function of $A$, mean annual rainfall, and $D_d$.
- **Soil-erosion modeling (USLE):** Slope length and steepness factors need $L_g$ and $\bar{S}$.
- **Watershed prioritization for soil conservation:** Composite ranking using $D_d$, $F_s$, $R_n$, $C_c$, $R_h$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops the *why* and *how* of each formula, the derivations of the geometric shape parameters, and demonstrates a worked-out numerical example end-to-end.

---

## 3.1 Derivation — Why Form Factor uses $L_b^2$ (Horton, 1932)

Horton defined the form factor as the ratio of basin area to the square of basin length.

**Step 1 — Take a circle of area A.**
Area of a circle of radius $r$ is $A = \pi r^2$, so $r = \sqrt{A / \pi}$.

**Step 2 — Define basin length $L_b$ for the circle.**
For the most "circular" basin, the longest dimension equals the diameter, i.e., $L_b = 2r$.

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum possible form factor.**

$$R_f^{max} = \frac{A}{L_b^2} = \frac{\pi r^2}{(2r)^2} = \frac{\pi r^2}{4 r^2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.785$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation.**
For any non-circular basin, the area is the same but the length $L_b$ is *longer* than the circular case, so the denominator increases and $R_f$ drops below 0.785. The closer $R_f$ is to 0.785, the more "circular" the basin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The maximum theoretical value of $R_f$ is $\pi/4 = 0.7854$, achieved only by a perfect circle. Real basins always show $R_f < 0.785$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Compactness Coefficient and Equivalence with Circularity

**Step 1 — Define the perimeter of a circle having area $A$.**

$$P_c = 2\sqrt{\pi A}$$

**Step 2 — Define the Compactness Coefficient.**

$$C_c = \frac{P}{P_c} = \frac{P}{2 \sqrt{\pi A}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the numerical coefficient.**

$$C_c = \frac{P}{2 \sqrt{\pi A}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\pi}} \cdot \frac{P}{\sqrt{A}} \approx 0.2821 \cdot \frac{P}{\sqrt{A}}$$

**Step 4 — Derive Circularity Ratio by inversion.**

$$R_c = \frac{P_c^{\,2}}{P^{\,2}} = \frac{4 \pi A}{P^{\,2}} = \frac{1}{C_c^{\,2}}$$

**Step 5 — Range check.**

- A perfect circle → $P = P_c$ → $C_c = 1$ → $R_c = 1$.
- An irregular elongated shape → $P > P_c$ → $C_c > 1$ → $R_c < 1$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** $C_c$ and $R_c$ convey the same information. Use $C_c \geq 1$ (often 1–3 in real basins) and $R_c \leq 1$ (often 0.2–0.8).

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Elongation Ratio (Schumm, 1956)

**Step 1 — Imagine a circle with the same area as the basin.**

Area $A = \pi D_c^{\,2} / 4 \;\Rightarrow\; D_c = 2\sqrt{A/\pi}$.

**Step 2 — Elongation Ratio is the diameter of this equivalent circle divided by the actual basin length.**

$$R_e = \frac{D_c}{L_b} = \frac{2\sqrt{A/\pi}}{L_b}$$

**Step 3 — Range check.**
- Perfect circle: $D_c = L_b$ → $R_e = 1$.
- Highly elongated basin: $D_c \ll L_b$ → $R_e \to 0$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Horton's Law of Stream Numbers

Horton observed that the number of streams of each order follows a geometric progression.

**Step 1 — Empirical observation.** Plot $\log N_u$ against $u$ (stream order). The points lie on a straight line, indicating a geometric series.

**Step 2 — Define the bifurcation ratio.**

$$R_b = \frac{N_u}{N_{u+1}} \quad \text{(typically constant for a basin)}$$

**Step 3 — Express the number of streams of order $u$ in terms of the highest order $k$ and $R_b$.**

$$N_u = R_b^{\,(k-u)}$$

**Step 4 — Verify for the highest order.** When $u = k$: $N_k = R_b^{\,0} = 1$ (true — there is exactly one main stream).

**Step 5 — Verify for $u = 1$.** $N_1 = R_b^{\,k-1}$, which is the largest count (correct — most streams are 1st order).

---

## 3.5 Derivation — Horton's Law of Stream Lengths

Empirically, mean stream length grows as a geometric function of order.

$$\bar{L}_u = \bar{L}_1 \cdot R_L^{\,(u-1)}$$

where $R_L = \bar{L}_{u+1} / \bar{L}_u$ is the length ratio, typically 1.5–3.5.

---

## 3.6 Derivation — Length of Overland Flow (L_g)

**Step 1 — Definition.** $L_g$ is the average horizontal distance from the drainage divide to the nearest stream channel.

**Step 2 — Consider two adjacent channels separated by a strip of width $2 L_g$.**

**Step 3 — Drainage density** is total stream length per unit area:

$$D_d = \frac{\text{total stream length}}{\text{area}} = \frac{1}{2 L_g}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $L_g$.**

$$L_g = \frac{1}{2 D_d}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** A highly dissected watershed (large $D_d$) has very short overland-flow paths. Runoff reaches the channel quickly → sharp hydrograph.

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Problem (Full Solution)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A watershed on a Survey of India topo-sheet has the following data:
>
> - Basin area: $A = 95.6 \text{ km}^2$
> - Perimeter: $P = 42.8 \text{ km}$
> - Basin length: $L_b = 17.5 \text{ km}$
> - Total stream length (all orders): $\Sigma L = 287 \text{ km}$
> - Maximum elevation: $810 \text{ m}$, Minimum elevation: $220 \text{ m}$
> - Stream count: $N_1 = 50$, $N_2 = 12$, $N_3 = 3$, $N_4 = 1$
>
> Compute: (a) Form factor, (b) Compactness coefficient, (c) Circularity ratio, (d) Elongation ratio, (e) Drainage density, (f) Stream frequency, (g) Bifurcation ratio, (h) Relief, (i) Relief ratio, (j) Length of overland flow.

### (a) Form Factor

$$R_f = \frac{A}{L_b^{\,2}} = \frac{95.6}{(17.5)^2}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
L_b^{\,2} &= 17.5 \times 17.5 = 306.25 \\
R_f &= \frac{95.6}{306.25} \approx 0.312
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment:** $R_f = 0.312$ is well below the circular maximum (0.785), indicating an **elongated basin**.

### (b) Compactness Coefficient

$$C_c = 0.2821 \cdot \frac{P}{\sqrt{A}} = 0.2821 \cdot \frac{42.8}{\sqrt{95.6}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{95.6} &\approx 9.777 \\
\frac{42.8}{9.777} &\approx 4.378 \\
C_c &\approx 0.2821 \times 4.378 \approx 1.235
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment:** $C_c = 1.235$ (close to 1) indicates a **moderately compact** basin.

### (c) Circularity Ratio

$$R_c = \frac{4 \pi A}{P^2} = \frac{4 \times 3.1416 \times 95.6}{(42.8)^2}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
4 \pi A &= 4 \times 3.1416 \times 95.6 \approx 1201.6 \\
P^2 &= 42.8^2 = 1831.84 \\
R_c &\approx \frac{1201.6}{1831.84} \approx 0.656
\end{aligned}$$

**Check:** $R_c = 1 / C_c^{\,2} = 1 / (1.235)^2 = 1 / 1.525 \approx 0.656$ ✓

### (d) Elongation Ratio

$$R_e = \frac{2 \sqrt{A/\pi}}{L_b} = \frac{2 \sqrt{95.6/3.1416}}{17.5}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
A/\pi &= 95.6 / 3.1416 \approx 30.43 \\
\sqrt{30.43} &\approx 5.516 \\
2 \times 5.516 &= 11.03 \\
R_e &= \frac{11.03}{17.5} \approx 0.631
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment:** $R_e = 0.631$ → **oval basin** (between 0.6 and 0.9).

### (e) Drainage Density

$$D_d = \frac{\Sigma L}{A} = \frac{287}{95.6} \approx 3.00 \text{ km/km}^2$$

**Comment:** $D_d = 3.0$ → **moderately high** drainage density. Suggests dense channel network, possibly with hard rock terrain.

### (f) Stream Frequency

$$F_s = \frac{\Sigma N}{A} = \frac{50 + 12 + 3 + 1}{95.6} = \frac{66}{95.6} \approx 0.690 \text{ streams/km}^2$$

### (g) Bifurcation Ratios

$$R_{b1} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{50}{12} \approx 4.17$$

$$R_{b2} = \frac{N_2}{N_3} = \frac{12}{3} = 4.00$$

$$R_{b3} = \frac{N_3}{N_4} = \frac{3}{1} = 3.00$$

$$\bar{R_b} = \frac{4.17 + 4.00 + 3.00}{3} \approx 3.72$$

**Comment:** $R_b \approx 3.7$ is in the typical 2–5 range, indicating a **well-developed dendritic** network.

### (h) Relief

$$H = 810 - 220 = 590 \text{ m}$$

### (i) Relief Ratio

$$R_h = \frac{H}{L_b} = \frac{590}{17.5} \approx 33.7 \text{ m/km}$$

**Comment:** $R_h = 33.7$ m/km → **moderately steep** basin (high relief relative to length).

### (j) Length of Overland Flow

$$L_g = \frac{1}{2 D_d} = \frac{1}{2 \times 3.00} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.167 \text{ km} = 167 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Synthesis:** This basin is **elongated, oval, moderately dissected, with a dense channel network and significant relief**. The peak-flow response will be somewhat delayed (elongation), but infiltration is limited due to dense drainage.

---

## 3.8 Quick Comparative Matrix of Shape Parameters

| Shape | $R_f$ | $C_c$ | $R_c$ | $R_e$ | Hydrologic Response |
|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|---------------------|
| **Perfect Circle** | 0.785 | 1.000 | 1.000 | 1.000 | Fastest, highest peak |
| **Oval** | 0.4–0.7 | 1.0–1.4 | 0.5–0.9 | 0.6–0.9 | Moderate |
| **Elongated** | 0.2–0.4 | 1.4–2.0 | 0.2–0.5 | 0.4–0.6 | Slow, delayed peak |
| **Highly Elongated** | < 0.2 | > 2.0 | < 0.2 | < 0.4 | Very slow, flat hydrograph |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023] Define Drainage Density. State its typical range and significance in watershed characterization.

**Model Answer:**

Drainage density ($D_d$) is defined as the **total length of all streams per unit area** of a watershed:

$$D_d = \frac{\Sigma L_u}{A}$$

where $\Sigma L_u$ is the sum of lengths of all streams (in km) and $A$ is the watershed area (in km²). Units: **km/km²**.

**Typical range:** $D_d$ varies from 0.5 km/km² in flat sedimentary terrain with high infiltration to about 5 km/km² in mountainous, impermeable, or highly dissected terrain.

**Significance:**
- Indicates the **closeness of stream spacing** and degree of dissection.
- High $D_d$ → dense network → rapid runoff response → sharp flood peaks and high erosion.
- Low $D_d$ → sparse network → prolonged travel time, more infiltration, more groundwater recharge.
- It is inversely related to **length of overland flow** ($L_g = 1 / 2D_d$).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] Differentiate between **Form Factor (R_f)** and **Elongation Ratio (R_e)**.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Form Factor (R_f) | Elongation Ratio (R_e) |
|--------|-------------------|------------------------|
| **Formula** | $R_f = A / L_b^2$ | $R_e = 2\sqrt{A/\pi} \,/\, L_b$ |
| **Reference** | Pure square of basin length | Diameter of circle of equal area |
| **Maximum value** | 0.785 (for a circle) | 1.0 (for a circle) |
| **Originator** | Horton (1932) | Schumm (1956) |
| **Range** | 0 to 0.785 | 0 to 1.0 |
| **Interpretation** | Lower = more elongated | Lower = more elongated |

Both quantify basin shape and elongate-ness. A circular basin yields maximum $R_f$ (0.785) and maximum $R_e$ (1.0); any deviation indicates elongation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> The following two questions are designed as alternatives. In a KTU ESE, the student will be expected to attempt **one** of these (or whichever combination the paper provides).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Define a watershed. List and explain any **four** shape parameters used in watershed characterization, with their formulas and significance. (7 marks)

**(b)** A watershed has area $A = 120 \text{ km}^2$, perimeter $P = 48 \text{ km}$, and basin length $L_b = 18 \text{ km}$. Calculate (i) Form Factor, (ii) Compactness Coefficient, (iii) Circularity Ratio, and (iv) Elongation Ratio. Comment on the shape of the basin. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Definition and Shape Parameters**

A **watershed** is a topographically enclosed geographic area within which all precipitated water drains to a common single outlet, separated from adjacent watersheds by a drainage divide.

The four principal shape parameters are:

1. **Form Factor (R_f) — Horton (1932):**
$$R_f = \frac{A}{L_b^{\,2}}$$
Range 0 to 0.785. Indicates compactness; circular basins yield $R_f = \pi/4 = 0.785$.

2. **Compactness Coefficient (C_c) — Gravelius (1914):**
$$C_c = 0.2821 \cdot \frac{P}{\sqrt{A}}$$
Always $\geq 1$. Equals 1 for a perfect circle.

3. **Circularity Ratio (R_c) — Miller (1953):**
$$R_c = \frac{4 \pi A}{P^2}$$
Range 0 to 1. Equals 1 for a perfect circle; equal to $1 / C_c^{\,2}$.

4. **Elongation Ratio (R_e) — Schumm (1956):**
$$R_e = \frac{2 \sqrt{A/\pi}}{L_b}$$
Range 0 to 1. Lower values → more elongated basins.

**Significance:** All four parameters predict the **basin hydrologic response**. Compact (high $R_f$, $R_e$, low $C_c$) basins produce **higher peak flows and shorter time of concentration**; elongated basins produce **flatter, delayed hydrographs**.

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation**

Given: $A = 120$ km², $P = 48$ km, $L_b = 18$ km.

**(i) Form Factor:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_f &= \frac{A}{L_b^{\,2}} \\
L_b^{\,2} &= 18 \times 18 = 324 \\
R_f &= \frac{120}{324} \approx 0.370
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Compactness Coefficient:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{A} &= \sqrt{120} \approx 10.954 \\
C_c &= 0.2821 \times \frac{48}{10.954} \\
&= 0.2821 \times 4.381 \\
&\approx 1.236
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Circularity Ratio:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_c &= \frac{4 \pi A}{P^2} = \frac{4 \times 3.1416 \times 120}{48^2} \\
&= \frac{1507.97}{2304} \approx 0.654
\end{aligned}$$

**Cross-check:** $1 / C_c^{\,2} = 1 / (1.236)^2 = 1 / 1.528 \approx 0.654$ ✓

**(iv) Elongation Ratio:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_e &= \frac{2 \sqrt{A/\pi}}{L_b} = \frac{2 \sqrt{120/3.1416}}{18} \\
&= \frac{2 \sqrt{38.197}}{18} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 6.180}{18} = \frac{12.36}{18} \approx 0.687
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment on Shape:**

- $R_f = 0.370$ (well below 0.785) → basin is **not circular**.
- $C_c = 1.236$ (close to 1) → **moderately compact**.
- $R_c = 0.654$ → moderately circular.
- $R_e = 0.687$ (between 0.6 and 0.9) → **oval shape**.

**Conclusion:** The basin is **oval, moderately compact, and mildly elongated**. The hydrologic response will show moderate peak flows with a reasonable time of concentration — not as sharp as a circular basin, but not as delayed as a strongly elongated one.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain the **Horton-Strahler method of stream ordering**. State and explain **Horton's Laws of Stream Numbers and Stream Lengths**. (7 marks)

**(b)** The following data is available for a third-order stream basin. Compute (i) Drainage Density, (ii) Stream Frequency, (iii) Bifurcation Ratios, and (iv) Length of Overland Flow.
- Total area $A = 50$ km²
- Total stream length: $L_1 = 30$ km (1st order), $L_2 = 12$ km (2nd order), $L_3 = 6$ km (3rd order)
- Number of streams: $N_1 = 25$, $N_2 = 6$, $N_3 = 1$ (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Stream Ordering and Horton's Laws**

**Horton-Strahler Stream Ordering:**

- A **1st-order stream** has no tributaries.
- A **2nd-order stream** is formed at the junction of **two 1st-order streams**.
- A higher-order stream is formed **only when two streams of the same order** meet.
- If a lower-order stream meets a higher-order stream, the order of the higher stream does **not** change.
- The **highest order** in the basin is the order of the main channel at the outlet.

**Horton's Law of Stream Numbers:** The number of streams of successive orders forms a geometric progression:

$$N_u = R_b^{\,(k - u)}$$

where $R_b$ is the bifurcation ratio (typically 2–5), $k$ is the highest order, and $u$ is the order under consideration. This law implies a constant ratio of stream counts between consecutive orders.

**Horton's Law of Stream Lengths:** The mean length of streams of successive orders also forms a geometric progression:

$$\bar{L}_u = \bar{L}_1 \cdot R_L^{\,(u-1)}$$

where $R_L = \bar{L}_{u+1} / \bar{L}_u$ is the length ratio, typically 1.5–3.5. The law captures the **self-similar** fractal-like organization of natural drainage networks.

**Significance:** Both laws enable **prediction of stream numbers and lengths in un-surveyed portions** of a basin and validate the **geometric similarity** of natural networks.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation**

Given: $A = 50$ km², $L_1 = 30$ km, $L_2 = 12$ km, $L_3 = 6$ km, $N_1 = 25$, $N_2 = 6$, $N_3 = 1$.

**(i) Drainage Density:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Sigma L &= L_1 + L_2 + L_3 = 30 + 12 + 6 = 48 \text{ km} \\
D_d &= \frac{\Sigma L}{A} = \frac{48}{50} = 0.96 \text{ km/km}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment:** $D_d = 0.96$ km/km² → **low to moderate** drainage density, indicating permeable sub-surface / gentle terrain.

**(ii) Stream Frequency:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Sigma N &= N_1 + N_2 + N_3 = 25 + 6 + 1 = 32 \\
F_s &= \frac{\Sigma N}{A} = \frac{32}{50} = 0.64 \text{ streams/km}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Bifurcation Ratios:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{b1} &= \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{25}{6} \approx 4.17 \\
R_{b2} &= \frac{N_2}{N_3} = \frac{6}{1} = 6.00 \\
\bar{R_b} &= \frac{4.17 + 6.00}{2} \approx 5.08
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment:** $R_{b2} = 6$ is **higher than the typical 2–5 range**, suggesting structural control (faults/joints) or a young, evolving drainage network.

**(iv) Length of Overland Flow:**

$$L_g = \frac{1}{2 D_d} = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.96} = \frac{1}{1.92} \approx 0.521 \text{ km} = 521 \text{ m}$$

**Comment:** A 521 m overland flow length is **fairly long** → slower runoff concentration → more infiltration, more delayed hydrograph.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warnings & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — Read Carefully!**

1. **Unit confusion in $D_d$:** $D_d$ has units **km/km²** (or m/m² if SI). Writing "km²/km" is wrong — costs **1 mark**.

2. **Confusing 1st-order and 2nd-order definitions:** Many students treat *any* small channel as 1st order and the main river as 2nd. The correct rule is: 2nd order forms *only* when **two 1st-order** channels meet.

3. **Forgetting the constant $0.2821$** in the Compactness Coefficient. Some derive the formula but skip the numerical evaluation.

4. **Reporting $R_f > 0.785$:** A red flag — implies incorrect measurement of $L_b$. Correct $L_b$ is the **longest straight-line** dimension, not the main channel length.

5. **Stating "Bifurcation ratio = 4" without showing work:** Always show $N_u / N_{u+1}$ for each pair and the average.

6. **Mixing up Form Factor and Elongation Ratio:** They are *not* the same. KTU exam questions often give both — write the **correct formula for each**.

7. **Forgetting the interpretation:** Numerical values alone are not enough. End with a **qualitative interpretation** (e.g., "the basin is oval, so peak flow will be moderate").

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Read this list twice before any exam on Module 1.**

### ✅ Core Definitions
- [ ] Watershed = topographically bounded area draining to a single outlet.
- [ ] Drainage divide = the boundary ridge of a watershed.
- [ ] Stream order = Horton-Strahler hierarchical classification.
- [ ] Drainage density = total stream length per unit area.

### ✅ Shape Parameters (Formulas + Limits)
- [ ] $R_f = A / L_b^{\,2}$ — max 0.785.
- [ ] $C_c = 0.2821 \cdot P / \sqrt{A}$ — always $\geq 1$.
- [ ] $R_c = 4 \pi A / P^2 = 1 / C_c^{\,2}$ — max 1.
- [ ] $R_e = 2\sqrt{A/\pi} \,/\, L_b$ — max 1.

### ✅ Drainage Network Parameters
- [ ] $D_d = \Sigma L / A$ — km/km².
- [ ] $F_s = N / A$ — 1/km².
- [ ] $R_b = N_u / N_{u+1}$ — dimensionless, typical 2–5.
- [ ] $L_g = 1 / (2 D_d)$ — km.
- [ ] $C = 1 / D_d$ — km²/km.

### ✅ Relief Parameters
- [ ] $H = E_{max} - E_{min}$ — in m.
- [ ] $R_h = H / L_b$ — in m/km.
- [ ] $R_n = H \cdot D_d$ — dimensionless.

### ✅ Horton's Laws
- [ ] Stream numbers: $N_u = R_b^{\,(k-u)}$.
- [ ] Stream lengths: $\bar{L}_u = \bar{L}_1 \cdot R_L^{\,(u-1)}$.

### ✅ Drainage Patterns
- [ ] Dendritic → uniform lithology.
- [ ] Trellis → folded sedimentary rocks.
- [ ] Rectangular → jointed/faulted rocks.
- [ ] Radial → volcanic dome.
- [ ] Annular → eroded dome.
- [ ] Parallel → uniform moderate slope.
- [ ] Centripetal → crater/closed basin.
- [ ] Deranged → glaciated terrain.

### ✅ Slope Methods
- [ ] Horton's: $\bar{S} = \Delta H \cdot \Sigma L / A$.
- [ ] Wentworth's: $\bar{S} = N \cdot \Delta H \cdot l / \Sigma L$.
- [ ] Area-weighted: $\bar{S} = \sum S_i A_i / \sum A_i$.

### ✅ Practical Interpretation Rules
- [ ] Higher $D_d$ → faster runoff, sharper peak.
- [ ] Compact basin (high $R_f$, $R_e$, low $C_c$) → high peak, short $T_c$.
- [ ] Elongated basin → delayed, attenuated peak.
- [ ] Higher $R_h$ → steeper basin, more erosion.
- [ ] Higher $R_b$ (above 5) → structural control / young drainage.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Watershed Characteristics**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrological Processes in Watersheds (Precipitation, Infiltration, Runoff)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET723 - WATERSHED CONSERVATION AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hydrological Processes in Watersheds (Precipitation, Infiltration, Runoff)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **watershed** (also called a drainage basin or catchment) is a topographically defined land area within which all precipitation that falls will eventually drain to a common outlet such as a river, lake, reservoir, or estuary. The **hydrological processes** operating within a watershed describe the complete movement, storage, and transformation of water as it cycles through the atmosphere, surface, and subsurface.

The three primary hydrological processes governing watershed response are:

- **Precipitation (P):** The total water delivered to the watershed surface from the atmosphere in any form — rain, snow, sleet, or hail. Measured in **millimeters (mm)** of equivalent water depth over the watershed area.
- **Infiltration (F):** The downward entry of water from the surface into the soil profile, governed by gravity and capillary forces. Expressed in **mm/hr (infiltration rate, f)** or **mm (cumulative infiltration depth, F)**.
- **Runoff (R):** The portion of precipitation that flows over the land surface toward streams, rivers, and eventually the watershed outlet. Expressed in **mm** of depth or **m³/s (discharge)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic directly addresses **CO1 (Module 1)** of OECET723 — *"Understand the fundamental hydrological processes governing watershed behaviour, including precipitation, infiltration, evaporation, and runoff generation."* Maps to Bloom's Level **L2 (Understand)** and **L3 (Apply)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Watershed as a "Water Factory"

Imagine a watershed as a giant, sloped **kitchen sink system**:

- **Precipitation** is the **faucet** turning on and pouring water into the sink.
- **Infiltration** is the **drain hole** at the bottom — how fast the sink can swallow water.
- **Runoff** is the water that **overflows** the sink because the drain cannot keep up, or because the faucet is pouring too hard.
- **Evapotranspiration** is the water that **evaporates** back into the air before it ever reaches the drain.

A well-vegetated, healthy watershed behaves like a sink with a **wide drain** (high infiltration) and a **tight seal** (low runoff). A degraded, urbanized watershed behaves like a sink with a **clogged drain** (low infiltration) and lots of **overflow** (flash runoff, flooding, erosion).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Precipitation depth | $P$ | **mm** | 0 – 2500 mm/year (Kerala: 2000–3500 mm/year) |
| Rainfall intensity | $i$ | **mm/hr** | 1 – 200 mm/hr |
| Infiltration rate | $f$ | **mm/hr** | 0 – 250 mm/hr (saturated: $f_c \approx 1$–5 mm/hr) |
| Cumulative infiltration | $F$ | **mm** | 0 – 500 mm/storm |
| Runoff depth | $R$ | **mm** | 0 – 1500 mm/year |
| Watershed area | $A$ | **km² or ha** | 1 ha – 10,000 km² |
| Peak discharge | $Q_p$ | **m³/s (cumecs)** | 0.1 – 100,000 m³/s |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | Constant |
| Density of water | $\rho_w$ | **1000 kg/m³** | Constant at 4 °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Kerala Context:** The state receives an average annual rainfall of **~3000 mm**, one of the highest in India. This makes infiltration and runoff estimation critical for flood management — a recurring KTU numerical problem theme.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Water Balance Equation — The Foundational Identity

Every watershed must obey the **conservation of mass**. Over any chosen time interval $\Delta t$:

$$P = R + I + ET + \Delta S + SG$$

Where:
- $P$ = Precipitation input (mm)
- $R$ = Surface runoff (mm)
- $I$ = Interflow / subsurface flow (mm)
- $ET$ = Evapotranspiration loss (mm)
- $\Delta S$ = Change in watershed storage (soil moisture, groundwater, depression) (mm)
- $SG$ = Sub-surface seepage / deep percolation loss (mm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **long-term annual averages**, $\Delta S \approx 0$ (storage returns to equilibrium), and the equation simplifies to $P \approx R + ET + SG$. This is the form most often used in KTU numerical problems.

### 2.2 Precipitation — Forms, Measurement, and Analysis

**Forms:** Rain, snow, hail, sleet, drizzle. In tropical Kerala, **rain is the dominant form**.

**Measurement devices:**
- **Non-recording:** Symon's gauge (standard rain gauge) — measures total depth only.
- **Recording:** Tipping-bucket rain gauge, weighing-type gauge, syphon-type (float) gauge — gives intensity vs. time (hyetograph).

**Key analytical tools:**
- **Mean precipitation over an area** — estimated by:
  - *Arithmetic Mean Method:* $\bar{P} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum P_i$ (simple, but inaccurate for non-uniform storms).
  - *Thiessen Polygon Method:* $\bar{P} = \dfrac{\sum A_i P_i}{\sum A_i}$ (weights each gauge by its polygon area).
  - *Isohyetal Method:* $\bar{P} = \dfrac{\sum A_i \bar{P_i}}{\sum A_i}$ between isohyets (most accurate, manual).

**Intensity–Duration–Frequency (IDF) Relationship:**

$$i = \dfrac{a}{(t + b)^c}$$

where $i$ = intensity (mm/hr), $t$ = duration (min or hr), and $a, b, c$ are regional empirical constants.

### 2.3 Infiltration — The Soil's Drinking Mechanism

Infiltration capacity $f_p$ (potential rate) decreases over time during a storm as soil pores fill, eventually approaching a constant **steady-state rate $f_c$** (the saturated hydraulic conductivity of the surface layer).

**The Three Governing Equations (must memorize for KTU):**

**(a) Horton's Equation (1933) — Empirical, most tested in exams:**

$$f(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c) e^{-kt}$$

Cumulative infiltration:

$$F(t) = f_c \, t + \dfrac{f_0 - f_c}{k}\left(1 - e^{-kt}\right)$$

where:
- $f_0$ = initial infiltration capacity (mm/hr)
- $f_c$ = final / steady-state capacity (mm/hr)
- $k$ = decay constant (1/hr)

**(b) Philip's Equation (1957) — Two-term physically based:**

$$f(t) = \dfrac{1}{2} S \, t^{-1/2} + K$$

$$F(t) = S \, t^{1/2} + K t$$

where $S$ = sorptivity, $K$ = hydraulic conductivity.

**(c) Green–Ampt Equation (1911) — Physically based, assumes sharp wetting front:**

$$F(t) = K \cdot t + \psi \, \Delta\theta \ln\left(1 + \dfrac{F(t)}{\psi \, \Delta\theta}\right)$$

Implicit in $F$ — requires iteration.

### 2.4 Runoff — The Overflow

**Components of runoff:**
1. **Hortonian overland flow** — occurs when rainfall intensity $i > f_p$ (infiltration capacity exceeded). Common in urban / paved areas.
2. **Saturation overland flow** — occurs when soil is fully saturated from below (rainforest / Kerala laterite).
3. **Subsurface storm flow / interflow** — lateral flow through unsaturated soil.

**Estimation methods:**

**(a) Rational Method (for small watersheds, $A < 50$ km²):**

$$Q_p = \dfrac{C \cdot I \cdot A}{360}$$

where $Q_p$ = peak discharge (m³/s), $C$ = runoff coefficient (dimensionless), $I$ = rainfall intensity (mm/hr) for time of concentration $t_c$, $A$ = area (hectares). [Note: 360 is the unit conversion factor when $A$ is in hectares and $I$ in mm/hr.]

**(b) SCS Curve Number (CN) Method (USDA, widely used):**

$$S = \dfrac{25400}{CN} - 254 \quad \text{(mm)}$$

$$I_a = 0.2 \, S$$

$$R = \dfrac{(P - 0.2 S)^2}{P + 0.8 S} \quad \text{for } P > 0.2S, \text{ else } R = 0$$

where $S$ = maximum potential retention, $I_a$ = initial abstraction, $CN$ = curve number (0–100, depends on land use, soil group, antecedent moisture).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = R + ET + I + \Delta S$ | Water balance (annual) | mm |
| 2 | $\bar{P} = \sum A_i P_i / \sum A_i$ | Thiessen mean rainfall | mm |
| 3 | $i = a/(t+b)^c$ | IDF curve | mm/hr |
| 4 | $f(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c) e^{-kt}$ | Horton infiltration rate | mm/hr |
| 5 | $F(t) = f_c t + (f_0 - f_c)(1 - e^{-kt})/k$ | Horton cumulative | mm |
| 6 | $f(t) = 0.5 S t^{-1/2} + K$ | Philip infiltration rate | mm/hr |
| 7 | $S = 25400/CN - 254$ | SCS retention | mm |
| 8 | $R = (P - 0.2S)^2 / (P + 0.8S)$ | SCS runoff depth | mm |
| 9 | $Q_p = C I A / 360$ | Rational peak discharge | m³/s, ha, mm/hr |
| 10 | $t_c = 0.01947 \cdot L^{0.77} / S^{0.385}$ | Kirpich time of concentration | min, m, m/m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The Rational Method and Horton Equation are the **two most repeated numericals** in Module 1 of OECET723 across previous KTU university exams. Memorize them with their exact unit conventions.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Stormwater drainage design** (urban catchments, culverts) → Rational Method.
- **Flood estimation for ungauged rural watersheds** → SCS-CN + Unit Hydrograph.
- **Irrigation and recharge planning** → Infiltration equations + water balance.
- **Reservoir sizing and spillway design** → IDF + runoff routing.
- **Climate-resilient agriculture in Kerala** → Water balance for paddy, coconut, rubber.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Horton's Infiltration Equation from First Principles

**Starting physical assumption:** The infiltration capacity decays exponentially from an initial maximum $f_0$ to a final equilibrium $f_c$ as the soil surface gets progressively wetted. We postulate:

$$f(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c) e^{-\alpha t}$$

**Step 1 — Define the decay.** Let $f(t) - f_c$ represent the "excess capacity" still available for infiltration. Horton's empirical law assumes this excess decays at a rate proportional to itself:

$$\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[f(t) - f_c\right] = -k \left[f(t) - f_c\right]$$

This is a first-order linear ODE. Separating variables and integrating from $t=0$ to $t$:

$$\int_{f_0 - f_c}^{f(t) - f_c} \dfrac{d(f - f_c)}{f - f_c} = -k \int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln\left[\dfrac{f(t) - f_c}{f_0 - f_c}\right] = -kt$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$\dfrac{f(t) - f_c}{f_0 - f_c} = e^{-kt}$$

Solving for $f(t)$:

$$f(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c) e^{-kt} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 2 — Cumulative infiltration.** $F(t) = \int_0^t f(\tau) \, d\tau$:

$$F(t) = \int_0^t \left[f_c + (f_0 - f_c) e^{-k\tau}\right] d\tau$$

$$F(t) = f_c t + (f_0 - f_c) \left[-\dfrac{1}{k} e^{-k\tau}\right]_0^t$$

$$F(t) = f_c t + (f_0 - f_c) \cdot \dfrac{1 - e^{-kt}}{k}$$

$$F(t) = f_c t + \dfrac{(f_0 - f_c)}{k}\left(1 - e^{-kt}\right) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 3 — Limiting behaviour check.**
- As $t \to 0$: $F(0) = 0$ ✓ (no infiltration at time zero).
- As $t \to \infty$: $F(\infty) \to f_c t + (f_0 - f_c)/k$ → linear with slope $f_c$ ✓.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: SCS Curve Number Runoff Equation

**Empirical basis:** From thousands of experimental plots, the USDA observed that the ratio of actual runoff to potential runoff equals the ratio of actual retention to maximum potential retention:

$$\dfrac{R}{P - I_a} = \dfrac{F_a}{S}$$

where $F_a$ = actual retention = $(P - I_a) - R$. Hence:

$$R = \dfrac{(P - I_a)^2}{P - I_a + S}$$

**Empirical simplification** — the standard initial abstraction approximation:

$$I_a = 0.2 \, S$$

Substituting:

$$R = \dfrac{(P - 0.2 S)^2}{P - 0.2 S + S} = \dfrac{(P - 0.2S)^2}{P + 0.8S} \quad \blacksquare$$

And $S$ is related to CN by definition (CN = 100 when $S = 0$, CN = 0 when $S = \infty$):

$$CN = \dfrac{25400}{S + 254} \implies S = \dfrac{25400}{CN} - 254 \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical (Horton's Infiltration)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024-Style Numerical Model:** A 60-minute storm falls on a watershed. Horton's parameters: $f_0 = 75$ mm/hr, $f_c = 5$ mm/hr, $k = 3.0$ per hour. Rainfall intensity is constant at $i = 50$ mm/hr. Find: (a) infiltration rate at $t = 30$ min, (b) total infiltration in 60 min, (c) total runoff.

**Solution:**

**(a) Infiltration rate at $t = 30$ min = 0.5 hr:**

$$f(0.5) = 5 + (75 - 5) e^{-3.0 \times 0.5}$$

$$f(0.5) = 5 + 70 \cdot e^{-1.5}$$

$$f(0.5) = 5 + 70 \times 0.2231 = 5 + 15.62 = 20.62 \text{ mm/hr}$$

**(b) Total infiltration in 60 min = 1 hr:**

$$F(1) = 5(1) + \dfrac{70}{3}(1 - e^{-3})$$

$$F(1) = 5 + 23.33 \times (1 - 0.0498)$$

$$F(1) = 5 + 23.33 \times 0.9502 = 5 + 22.17 = 27.17 \text{ mm}$$

**(c) Total runoff:**

Total rainfall depth = $i \times t = 50 \times 1 = 50$ mm. Applying water balance:

$$R = P - F = 50 - 27.17 = 22.83 \text{ mm}$$

**Ponding time check:** $f(t) = i$ when $5 + 70 e^{-3t} = 50$ → $e^{-3t} = 0.6429$ → $t_p = 0.147$ hr ≈ 8.8 min. After ponding, runoff begins.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical (SCS-CN Method)

> A small agricultural watershed with **Hydrologic Soil Group B**, continuous fallow cover, and **AMC II** has a curve number $CN = 78$. A storm delivers $P = 120$ mm of rainfall in 24 hours. Compute: (a) maximum retention $S$, (b) initial abstraction $I_a$, (c) runoff depth $R$.

**Solution:**

**(a) Maximum retention:**

$$S = \dfrac{25400}{78} - 254 = 325.64 - 254 = 71.64 \text{ mm}$$

**(b) Initial abstraction:**

$$I_a = 0.2 \times 71.64 = 14.33 \text{ mm}$$

**(c) Runoff depth** (since $P = 120 > 0.2 S = 14.33$):

$$R = \dfrac{(120 - 14.33)^2}{120 - 14.33 + 71.64} = \dfrac{(105.67)^2}{177.31}$$

$$R = \dfrac{11166.15}{177.31} = 62.97 \text{ mm} \approx 63 \text{ mm}$$

**Runoff coefficient** = $R/P = 63/120 = 0.525$ (52.5% of rain became runoff).

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hydrological Cycle Block Diagram — Watershed System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ATM[Atmospheric Moisture] -->|Condensation + Lifting| CLD[Cloud Formation]
    CLD -->|Precipitation P| SURF[Watershed Surface]
    SURF -->|Interception I_nc| VEG[Vegetation Canopy]
    VEG -->|Evaporation E| ATM
    SURF -->|Depression Storage D_s| DEP[Ponded Water]
    DEP -->|Evaporation E| ATM
    SURF -->|Infiltration f| SOIL[Soil Zone]
    SOIL -->|Capillary Rise| RZ[Root Zone Uptake]
    RZ -->|Transpiration T| ATM
    SOIL -->|Percolation P_c| GW[Groundwater Aquifer]
    GW -->|Base Flow B_f| STRM[Stream Channel]
    GW -->|Deep Seepage SG| LOSS[Deep Loss]
    SURF -->|Hortonian Overland Flow| OF1[Overland Flow R_o]
    SOIL -->|Interflow| OF2[Subsurface Flow I_f]
    OF1 --> STRM
    OF2 --> STRM
    STRM -->|Channel Runoff R| OUT[Watershed Outlet Q]
    DEP -->|Saturation Excess| OF1
```

**Reading the diagram:** All arrows into the watershed surface (precipitation $P$) must equal the sum of all arrows leaving (runoff $R$ + evapotranspiration $ET$ + infiltration to deep storage $SG$ + change in storage $\Delta S$) — this is the water balance visually.

### 4.2 Process Interaction — State-Transition Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rain Starts: t = 0] --> B{f compare i}
    B -->|f greater i| C[All rainfall infiltrates]
    B -->|f less i| D[Ponding Begins at t_p]
    C --> E[Soil wetting front advances]
    D --> F[Surface detention builds]
    E --> G[After ponding, runoff starts]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Infiltration rate decays to f_c]
    H --> I[Rain stops]
    I --> J[Continued infiltration from detention]
    J --> K[All excess flows to channel]
    K --> L[Hydrograph Rises, Peaks, Recedes]
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture — Precipitation → Infiltration → Runoff Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Input Data: P, t, f_0, f_c, k, CN, A, C] --> STEP1{Choose Method}
    STEP1 -->|Infiltration Rate| HORT[Horton f of t]
    STEP1 -->|Cumulative F| HCUM[F of t Integration]
    STEP1 -->|Runoff Depth| SCS[SCS-CN Formula]
    STEP1 -->|Peak Discharge| RAT[Rational Formula]
    HORT --> COMP[Compare f t with i]
    HCUM --> WB[Water Balance R = P minus F]
    COMP -->|f less i| RUN[Generate Runoff Volume]
    COMP -->|f greater i| ZERO[Runoff equals zero]
    SCS --> RUN
    WB --> RUN
    RAT --> PEAK[Output Q_p m cubed per s]
    RUN --> OUT[Output R mm and Volume m cubed]
    ZERO --> OUT
    PEAK --> DESIGN[Engineering Design Yield]
    OUT --> DESIGN
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram design rationale:** These flowcharts mimic the "process interaction maps" used in KTU Module 1 tutorials. They trace the logical path a student must follow when solving combined precipitation-infiltration-runoff numericals — a key exam skill.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]**
> Define the term **watershed**. List the three primary hydrological processes operating within a watershed and state the fundamental water balance equation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A watershed is a topographically delineated land area that collects precipitation and drains it through a common outlet such as a river or lake. The three primary hydrological processes are: **(i) Precipitation (P)** — atmospheric water delivery to the surface, **(ii) Infiltration (F)** — downward entry of water into soil, and **(iii) Runoff (R)** — lateral surface flow to streams. The water balance equation is:

$$P = R + I + ET + \Delta S + SG$$

In long-term annual form: $P = R + ET + SG$ when $\Delta S \to 0$. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing processes, 1 mark for water balance equation.)*

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]**
> Differentiate between **Hortonian overland flow** and **saturation overland flow**. In which type of watershed (urban or forested) is each dominant?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Hortonian overland flow** occurs when rainfall intensity $i$ exceeds the infiltration capacity $f_p$ of the soil. It is dominant in **urbanized / paved watersheds** where the surface is impermeable. **Saturation overland flow** occurs when the soil becomes saturated from below (e.g., rising water table) and can absorb no more water, regardless of rainfall intensity. It is dominant in **humid, forested watersheds** with shallow water tables, such as the laterite catchments of Kerala. *(1.5 marks for Hortonian + 1.5 marks for saturation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / 2023 pattern]**
> **(a)** Derive **Horton's infiltration equation** starting from the first-order decay assumption. State clearly the physical meaning of each parameter. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a watershed storm, Horton's parameters are: $f_0 = 60$ mm/hr, $f_c = 6$ mm/hr, $k = 2.5$ hr⁻¹. The storm has constant intensity $i = 40$ mm/hr for a duration of **90 minutes**.
>     (i) Compute the **ponding time** $t_p$.
>     (ii) Compute the **total infiltration** during the 90-minute storm.
>     (iii) Compute the **total runoff depth** assuming no other losses. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (A):**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
Assumption: excess infiltration capacity decays as first-order kinetics.

$$\dfrac{d}{dt}[f(t) - f_c] = -k[f(t) - f_c]$$

Integrating and applying $f(0) = f_0$:

$$f(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c) e^{-kt}$$

- $f_0$ = initial infiltration capacity at $t=0$ (mm/hr).
- $f_c$ = final steady-state infiltration rate (mm/hr) = saturated hydraulic conductivity of surface.
- $k$ = decay constant (1/hr), reflecting how fast soil surface seals.
- $f(t)$ = infiltration capacity at time $t$ (mm/hr).

Cumulative form: $F(t) = f_c t + \dfrac{f_0 - f_c}{k}(1 - e^{-kt})$. *(2 marks for setup + ODE, 2 marks for integration, 2 marks for final form, 1 mark for parameter meaning.)*

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Ponding time:** $f(t_p) = i$ → $6 + 54 e^{-2.5 t_p} = 40$ → $e^{-2.5 t_p} = 34/54 = 0.6296$ → $t_p = -\ln(0.6296)/2.5 = 0.462$ hr = **27.7 minutes**.

**(ii) Total infiltration in 90 min = 1.5 hr:**

$$F(1.5) = 6(1.5) + \dfrac{54}{2.5}(1 - e^{-2.5 \times 1.5})$$

$$F(1.5) = 9 + 21.6(1 - e^{-3.75}) = 9 + 21.6(1 - 0.02352)$$

$$F(1.5) = 9 + 21.6 \times 0.9765 = 9 + 21.09 = 30.09 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Total runoff depth:** Total rainfall $= 40 \times 1.5 = 60$ mm.

$$R = P - F = 60 - 30.09 = 29.91 \text{ mm} \approx 29.9 \text{ mm}$$ *(2 marks + 3 marks + 2 marks distribution.)*

---

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam — May 2023 pattern — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**
> **(a)** Explain the **SCS Curve Number method** for estimating runoff depth. Derive the runoff equation $R = (P - 0.2S)^2/(P + 0.8S)$ and define the role of the curve number $CN$. List the four **Hydrologic Soil Groups (A, B, C, D)** with one characteristic for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A small agricultural catchment in Thrissur (Kerala) has area $A = 12$ hectares, hydrologic soil group **B**, land use = row crops with straight row treatment, and **AMC II**. A 6-hour storm delivers $P = 95$ mm.
>     (i) Determine the **curve number $CN$** and retention $S$.
>     (ii) Compute the **runoff depth $R$**.
>     (iii) If the rational method runoff coefficient for this area is $C = 0.35$ and the rainfall intensity for $t_c = 30$ min is $I = 70$ mm/hr, compute the **peak discharge $Q_p$**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (B):**

**(a) Theory (7 marks):**
The SCS-CN method (USDA, 1954) is an empirical procedure to estimate direct runoff from storm rainfall using a **Curve Number (CN)** that depends on soil type, land use, hydrologic condition, and antecedent moisture condition (AMC). The basic proportionality is:

$$\dfrac{R}{P - I_a} = \dfrac{F_a}{S}$$

with empirical relation $I_a = 0.2S$, leading to $R = (P - 0.2S)^2/(P + 0.8S)$. The relation $S = 25400/CN - 254$ (mm) links retention to CN. *(2 marks for proportionality, 2 marks for derivation of R formula, 1 mark for CN definition.)*

**Hydrologic Soil Groups:**
- **Group A:** Deep sand, deep loess, aggregated silts → high infiltration (>7.5 mm/hr), low runoff potential.
- **Group B:** Shallow loess, sandy loam → moderate infiltration (3.8–7.5 mm/hr).
- **Group C:** Clay loams, shallow sandy loam, soils low in organic matter → slow infiltration (1.3–3.8 mm/hr).
- **Group D:** Heavy plastic clays, saline soils, deep tight clays → very slow infiltration (<1.3 mm/hr), high runoff potential. *(2 marks for soil group listing.)*

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) CN and S:** From the standard SCS-CN table, **row crops, straight row, HSG-B, AMC II** → **CN = 78**.

$$S = \dfrac{25400}{78} - 254 = 325.64 - 254 = 71.64 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Runoff depth** (since $P = 95 > 0.2S = 14.33$):

$$R = \dfrac{(95 - 14.33)^2}{95 + 57.31} = \dfrac{(80.67)^2}{152.31} = \dfrac{6507.65}{152.31} = 42.72 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Peak discharge** (Rational Method):

$$Q_p = \dfrac{C \cdot I \cdot A}{360} = \dfrac{0.35 \times 70 \times 12}{360} = \dfrac{294}{360} = 0.817 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ *(2 + 3 + 2 marks distribution.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in Rational Method** — using $A$ in km² instead of hectares without adjusting the 360 constant. Marks deducted: 1–2.
> 2. **Forgetting ponding time** — students apply Horton's equation from $t=0$ even when $i > f_0$. Marks deducted: 2–3.
> 3. **SCS-CN check** — failing to verify $P > 0.2S$ before applying the runoff formula. Marks deducted: 1.
> 4. **Sign convention in water balance** — using positive values for losses inconsistently. Marks deducted: 1.
> 5. **Exponential decay — natural log vs log base 10** — confusing $e$ with 10 in Horton's equation. Marks deducted: 2.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Watershed = drainage area** bounded by ridge lines, draining to a common outlet.
- [x] **Water balance:** $P = R + ET + I + \Delta S + SG$ (annual: $P \approx R + ET$).
- [x] **Precipitation forms:** rain, snow, hail, sleet. In Kerala → rain dominant.
- [x] **Thiessen polygons** for mean areal rainfall (weights = polygon area).
- [x] **Horton's equation:** $f(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c)e^{-kt}$ — parameters: $f_0$ initial, $f_c$ final, $k$ decay constant.
- [x] **Philip's equation:** $f(t) = 0.5 S t^{-1/2} + K$ — sorptivity $S$, conductivity $K$.
- [x] **Green–Ampt:** assumes sharp wetting front, implicit in $F$.
- [x] **Infiltration capacity $f_p$ decreases** with time and approaches $f_c$.
- [x] **Ponding time $t_p$** = time when $f(t_p) = i$ (rainfall intensity). After $t_p$, runoff begins.
- [x] **Runoff types:** Hortonian ($i > f_p$, urban) and saturation (forested, water-table high).
- [x] **Rational method:** $Q_p = C \cdot I \cdot A / 360$ — for $A < 50$ km².
- [x] **SCS-CN:** $S = 25400/CN - 254$, $I_a = 0.2S$, $R = (P-0.2S)^2/(P+0.8S)$.
- [x] **Soil groups A–D** = high → very low infiltration.
- [x] **Antecedent Moisture Condition (AMC) I, II, III** = dry, average, wet.
- [x] **Time of concentration $t_c$** = Kirpich: $0.01947 L^{0.77}/S^{0.385}$ (min, m, m/m).
- [x] **Kerala context:** 3000 mm/yr rainfall, laterite soils, frequent flooding → runoff and infiltration estimation is **mandatory exam literacy**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** "Water balance first, then estimate each component. Always state units. Always check ponding time. Always verify SCS-CN applicability ($P > 0.2S$)."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance of Watershed Properties: Effect of Physical Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET723 - WATERSHED CONSERVATION AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Importance of Watershed Properties: Effect of Physical Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **watershed** (also called a *drainage basin* or *catchment*) is a **geo-hydrological unit** of land surface defined by a *drainage divide*, within which all precipitation received converges to a single *outlet* (pour point) through a network of streams, overland flow paths, and subsurface flow. A watershed is an *open system* exchanging energy and mass (water, sediment, nutrients) with the atmosphere, lithosphere, and biosphere.

The **physical properties** of a watershed — sometimes called *morphometric parameters* or *basin characteristics* — are the **measurable geometric, topographic, and surface attributes** that collectively govern the catchment's hydrologic response. They are classified into three broad groups:

| Group | Property Type | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Linear** | One-dimensional length measurements | Stream order, stream length, basin length, perimeter |
| **Areal (Shape & Size)** | Two-dimensional surface measurements | Area, drainage density, stream frequency, form factor, elongation ratio, circularity ratio |
| **Relief (3D)** | Vertical / volumetric measurements | Maximum relief, relief ratio, relative relief, ruggedness number |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Module 1 explicitly demands the student to be able to *"appreciate the importance of physical properties of a watershed in influencing runoff, infiltration, soil erosion, peak flow, and time of concentration."* This topic is foundational — almost every later module (rainfall-runoff modelling, soil conservation, reservoir sedimentation) depends on these properties.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Funnel-and-Sponge" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Bath-Tub Funnel"**
> Imagine a giant bath-tub. The physical properties of a watershed behave like the *shape, slope, surface texture, and size of that tub*:
> - A **large, gently-sloping, smooth tub** drains *slowly* (low peak flow, long time of concentration).
> - A **small, steep, rough tub** drains *quickly* (high peak flow, short lag time).
> - The **drainage network** is like the *plumbing* — more pipes (high drainage density) mean water exits faster.
> - The **shape** decides whether all water arrives at the outlet *simultaneously* (peaked hydrograph) or *sequentially* (flattened hydrograph).

The **physical properties are the knobs and dials** of this bath-tub. A watershed manager's job is to understand each knob, then adjust the land use (the *surface roughness* knob) and structures (the *drainage path* knob) to *moderate* the response.

## 1.3 Why "Physical Properties" Are Foundational

The fundamental hydrologic equation that a watershed satisfies is the **Water Balance Equation**:

$$
P = R + ET + \Delta S + G
$$

where:
- $P$ = Precipitation (input)
- $R$ = Surface runoff (output at outlet)
- $ET$ = Evapotranspiration (atmospheric loss)
- $\Delta S$ = Change in storage (soil moisture, groundwater, surface storage)
- $G$ = Deep groundwater outflow

The **physical properties of the watershed dictate how the input $P$ is partitioned** between $R$, $ET$, $\Delta S$, and $G$. Two watersheds receiving **identical rainfall** will produce *vastly different* hydrographs solely because of their physical characteristics.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metric Used Globally:** Physical properties are typically extracted from **Survey of India (SoI) toposheets** at 1:50,000 scale (contour interval 20 m) and from **Digital Elevation Models (DEMs)** of resolution $\leq 30 \text{ m}$ (e.g., SRTM, ASTER, CARTOSAT). For KTU field projects, students are expected to know both manual and GIS-based extraction methods.
