# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Physical Properties

The watershed characteristics that control hydrological response are systematically grouped below.

### A. Size (Area)
The **area of a watershed** $A$ (in $\text{km}^2$) is the most fundamental descriptor. It has an **inverse relationship with many response variables** because larger catchments offer more storage opportunity (in depressions, soil, channel banks).

**Hydrologic effects of size:**
- **Peak discharge per unit area** decreases as area increases.
- **Base flow contribution** increases with area (more groundwater storage).
- **Time of concentration** $T_c$ increases with area.
- **Runoff coefficient** may increase (if rainfall variability decreases) or stay roughly constant.

### B. Shape
The **shape of a watershed** determines the *time-distribution* of runoff contribution at the outlet. A **fan-shaped (or semi-circular)** watershed delivers water from all overland-flow paths **nearly simultaneously**, producing a **high, sharp hydrograph peak**. A **long, narrow (elongated)** watershed delivers water in *staggered* fashion, producing a **lower, broader hydrograph**.

**Shape parameters (derived from area $A$ and basin length $L_b$):**

- **Form Factor** (Horton, 1932): $R_f = \dfrac{A}{L_b^2}$
- **Elongation Ratio**: $R_e = \dfrac{1.128 \sqrt{A}}{L_b} = \dfrac{2}{L_b}\sqrt{\dfrac{A}{\pi}}$
- **Circularity Ratio** (Miller, 1953): $R_c = \dfrac{4\pi A}{P^2}$
- **Compactness Coefficient** (Gravelius, 1914): $C_c = \dfrac{P}{2\sqrt{\pi A}} = \dfrac{0.2821\, P}{\sqrt{A}}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Guide:**
> - $R_f = 0.78$ for a *perfect circle* (theoretical maximum).
> - $R_f \to 0$ for a *highly elongated* watershed.
> - $C_c = 1$ for a *perfect circle*; $C_c > 1$ indicates *deviation from circularity*.
> - Higher $R_c$ (closer to 1) ⇒ more circular ⇒ **higher peak, shorter lag**.

### C. Slope (Topography)
Slope $S$ controls the **velocity of overland and channel flow**, and hence $T_c$, the **infiltration opportunity time**, and **erosion intensity**.

**Slope parameters:**
- **Average basin slope** $\bar{S}$ (weighted by hypsometric curve).
- **Channel slope** $S_{ch} = \dfrac{\Delta H}{L_{ch}}$
- **Relief** $H = H_{max} - H_{min}$ (m).
- **Relief Ratio** $R_h = \dfrac{H}{L_b}$ (dimensionless).
- **Relative Relief** $R_{rel} = \dfrac{H}{P}$ (m/km of perimeter).
- **Ruggedness Number** $R_n = D_d \times H$ (Strahler, 1958).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ruggedness Number** is a *combined* parameter that captures both *dissection* (drainage density $D_d$) and *relief* ($H$). High $R_n$ watersheds are *vulnerable to flash floods and landslides* — extremely relevant in the **Western Ghats of Kerala**.

### D. Drainage Network Parameters
- **Drainage Density** $D_d = \dfrac{\sum L_i}{A}$ ($\text{km/km}^2$) — total stream length per unit area.
- **Stream Frequency** $F_s = \dfrac{N_s}{A}$ ($\text{streams/km}^2$) — number of streams per unit area.
- **Infiltration Number** $I_f = D_d \times F_s$.
- **Length of Overland Flow** $L_o = \dfrac{1}{2 D_d}$.
- **Constant of Channel Maintenance** $C = \dfrac{1}{D_d}$ (area needed to sustain 1 km of channel).
- **Drainage Texture** $T = \dfrac{N_s}{P}$ (streams/km of perimeter).
- **Stream Order** $u$ (Strahler, 1957) — hierarchical ordering.

### E. Relief & Hypsometry
- **Hypsometric Curve / Integral** — area-elevation distribution.
- **Dissection Index** $D_i = \dfrac{H}{H_{max}}$.
- **Mass-elevation Curve** — used to assess *erosional maturity* (youthful, mature, old-stage watersheds).

### F. Surface Properties (Soil, Geology, Land Use)
Although not strictly "morphometric", these control **infiltration capacity** $f_c$, **Manning's roughness** $n$, and **surface storage**:
- **Soil type & depth** (sandy → high $f_c$; clay → low $f_c$).
- **Geology** (fractured basalt → high infiltration; massive granite → low).
- **Land use/cover** (forest → high interception & $f_c$; urban → low & fast).
- **Surface roughness** (quantified via Manning's $n$).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas. They are the *de-facto* toolbox for any KTU morphometry problem.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Typical Range (Kerala) |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Drainage Density | $D_d = \dfrac{\sum L_i}{A}$ | $\sum L_i$ = total stream length | $\text{km/km}^2$ | 1.0 – 6.0 |
| 2 | Stream Frequency | $F_s = \dfrac{N_s}{A}$ | $N_s$ = number of streams | $\text{no./km}^2$ | 0.5 – 6.0 |
| 3 | Infiltration Number | $I_f = D_d \times F_s$ | Combined texture | $\text{km}^{-2}$ | 0.5 – 30 |
| 4 | Form Factor | $R_f = \dfrac{A}{L_b^2}$ | $L_b$ = basin length | dimensionless | 0.1 – 0.8 |
| 5 | Elongation Ratio | $R_e = \dfrac{1.128\sqrt{A}}{L_b}$ | Same as above | dimensionless | 0.4 – 1.0 |
| 6 | Circularity Ratio | $R_c = \dfrac{4\pi A}{P^2}$ | $P$ = perimeter | dimensionless | 0.2 – 0.9 |
| 7 | Compactness Coefficient | $C_c = \dfrac{0.2821\, P}{\sqrt{A}}$ | Gravelius index | dimensionless | 1.0 – 3.0 |
| 8 | Length of Overland Flow | $L_o = \dfrac{1}{2 D_d}$ | Horton (1945) | $\text{km}$ | 0.08 – 0.5 |
| 9 | Relief Ratio | $R_h = \dfrac{H}{L_b}$ | $H$ = basin relief | dimensionless | 0.001 – 0.5 |
| 10 | Relative Relief | $R_{rel} = \dfrac{H}{P}$ | Relief per perimeter | $\text{m/km}$ | 10 – 200 |
| 11 | Ruggedness Number | $R_n = D_d \times H$ | Strahler (1958) | dimensionless | 0.05 – 5.0 |
| 12 | Drainage Texture | $T = \dfrac{N_s}{P}$ | Smith (1950) | $\text{no./km}$ | 1 – 10 |
| 13 | Time of Concentration (Kirpich) | $T_c = \dfrac{0.01947\, L^{0.77}}{S^{0.385}}$ | $L$ in m, $S$ = slope | $\text{min}$ | 5 – 60 min |
| 14 | Time of Concentration (Kerby) | $T_c = \dfrac{0.83\, (L \cdot n)^{0.467}}{S^{0.235}}$ | $n$ = roughness | $\text{min}$ | 5 – 90 min |
| 15 | Lag Time | $t_p \approx 0.6\, T_c$ | For triangular UH | $\text{min}$ | — |
| 16 | Bifurcation Ratio | $R_b = \dfrac{N_u}{N_{u+1}}$ | $N_u$ = streams of order $u$ | dimensionless | 3 – 5 |
| 17 | Length Ratio | $R_L = \dfrac{\bar{L}_u}{\bar{L}_{u-1}}$ | Mean length ratio | dimensionless | 1.5 – 3.5 |
| 18 | Areal Ratio | $R_a = \dfrac{\bar{A}_u}{\bar{A}_{u-1}}$ | Mean area ratio | dimensionless | 3 – 6 |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of These Parameters

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Applications — Where These Formulas Are Used in Practice:**
> 1. **Flood Estimation (Rational Method):** $Q_p = C\, I\, A$ — uses *area* $A$ directly, and *runoff coefficient* $C$ depends on land use/soil/slope.
> 2. **Unit Hydrograph Derivation:** Clark's and Snyder's synthetic UHs use $T_c$, $L_b$, $R_f$, $C_c$.
> 3. **Reservoir Capacity Planning:** Sediment yield estimation uses $R_n$, $D_d$, $R_h$.
> 4. **Watershed Prioritization for Conservation:** Morphometric ranking of sub-watersheds uses $D_d$, $F_s$, $R_n$, $R_f$ to identify *erosion-prone* priority areas.
> 5. **Landslide Susceptibility Mapping (Kerala Context):** High $R_n$ + high $D_d$ = *high landslide risk* — used by KSDMA (Kerala State Disaster Management Authority).
> 6. **Stormwater Drainage Design:** $T_c$ computed via Kirpich/Kerby governs pipe sizing in urban Kerala municipalities.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Horton Form Factor $R_f$

**Starting point:** Horton's (1932) reasoning that a *circular watershed* of the same area produces the *fastest* runoff concentration, because every point is equidistant from the centre.

**Step 1:** A *perfect circle* has area $A_c = \pi r_c^2$, hence radius $r_c = \sqrt{A/\pi}$.

**Step 2:** The *longest flow path* in a circle is the diameter, $L_c = 2r_c = 2\sqrt{A/\pi}$.

**Step 3:** The *Form Factor* is defined as the ratio of basin area to the square of basin length, normalised so that a *circular basin* gives the *maximum* value:

$$
R_f = \frac{A}{L_b^2}
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting the *circular* case ($A$ = circle area, $L_b = 2\sqrt{A/\pi}$):

$$
R_f^{\text{circle}} = \frac{A}{\left(2\sqrt{A/\pi}\right)^2} = \frac{A}{4A/\pi} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.7854
$$

**Interpretation:** $R_f$ ranges from **0 (highly elongated)** to **0.7854 (circular)**. Lower $R_f$ ⇒ longer, narrower basin ⇒ *delayed, flattened* hydrograph. Higher $R_f$ ⇒ more compact ⇒ *sharper, earlier* peak.

## 3.2 Derivation: The Strahler Ruggedness Number $R_n$

Strahler (1958) sought a *single dimensionless index* that captures *both* the *fineness of dissection* and the *steepness* of a watershed.

**Step 1:** Define *drainage density* (horizontal measure of dissection):

$$
D_d = \frac{\sum L_i}{A} \quad [\text{km/km}^2]
$$

**Step 2:** Define *basin relief* (vertical measure of dissection):

$$
H = H_{\max} - H_{\min} \quad [\text{m}]
$$

**Step 3:** To combine them into a *dimensionless* number, Strahler multiplied them, recognising that:

- High $D_d$ alone (a flat, highly-dissected plain) gives mild terrain.
- High $H$ alone (a steep, undissected plateau) gives moderate terrain.
- **High $D_d$ AND high $H$ together** give *extreme, rugged terrain* prone to flash floods.

**Step 4:** Therefore the *Ruggedness Number* is:

$$
R_n = D_d \times H
$$

**Step 5:** Dimensional check:
- $D_d$: $\text{km/km}^2$ = $1/\text{km}$
- $H$: $\text{km}$ (if we convert metres to km for consistency)
- Product: $(1/\text{km}) \times \text{km} = $ **dimensionless**. ✓

**Note:** In practice, if $D_d$ is in $\text{km/km}^2$ and $H$ is in **metres**, $R_n$ carries the unit $\text{m/km}$ and is *not strictly dimensionless*. Always report $H$ in **km** for true dimensionless $R_n$.

## 3.3 Derivation: The Kirpich Time of Concentration Formula

The Kirpich (1940) equation is the most widely used $T_c$ formula in small agricultural and urban watersheds (used in **CSRS, Kerala**, and **CPWD drainage design**).

**Empirical origin:** Kirpich fitted data from seven small rural catchments (0.005 to $0.4 \text{ km}^2$) in Tennessee, USA, using the basic kinematic-wave assumption that:

$$
T_c \propto \frac{L^{a}}{S^{b}}
$$

**Step 1:** He determined the exponents by **log-log regression** on observed $T_c$ vs. $L/S$:

$$
\log T_c = \log k + a \log L - b \log S
$$

**Step 2:** Best-fit values from regression: $a = 0.77$, $b = 0.385$, $k = 0.01947$ (when $L$ in metres, $S$ in m/m, $T_c$ in minutes).

**Step 3:** Final empirical equation:

$$
T_c = \frac{0.01947\, L^{0.77}}{S^{0.385}}
$$

where:
- $L$ = maximum length of travel of water (m)
- $S$ = average slope along flow path (m/m)
- $T_c$ = time of concentration (min)

**Worked numerical example:**

Suppose a watershed has $L = 1500 \text{ m}$ and average slope $S = 0.02$ (2 %).

**Step 1:** Compute $L^{0.77}$:

$$
L^{0.77} = 1500^{0.77}
$$

Take logarithms:
$$
\log_{10}(1500) = 3.1761
$$
$$
0.77 \times 3.1761 = 2.4456
$$
$$
10^{2.4456} = 278.96
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $S^{0.385}$:

$$
\log_{10}(0.02) = -1.6990
$$
$$
0.385 \times (-1.6990) = -0.6541
$$
$$
10^{-0.6541} = 0.2216
$$

**Step 3:** Assemble:

$$
T_c = \frac{0.01947 \times 278.96}{0.2216} = \frac{5.4308}{0.2216} \approx 24.51 \text{ min}
$$

**Interpretation:** Runoff from the most remote point of this 1.5 km watershed takes about **25 minutes** to reach the outlet — used to size the *time-step* of the design storm hydrograph.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Each Property on Hydrologic Response

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this matrix:**
> "↑" = property *increases*; "↓" = property *decreases*; "→" = causes/affects.

| Physical Property | Increase → Runoff (R) | Increase → Peak Discharge ($Q_p$) | Increase → Erosion | Increase → $T_c$ / Lag |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Area** ↑ | Slight ↑ | ↑ (but $Q_p/A$ ↓) | Variable | ↑ (longer flow paths) |
| **Form Factor** $R_f$ ↑ | ↑ | ↑ (sharper peak) | ↑ | ↓ (shorter, faster) |
| **Elongation Ratio** $R_e$ ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |
| **Circularity** $R_c$ ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |
| **Drainage Density** $D_d$ ↑ | ↑ (faster delivery) | ↑ | ↑ (more channels) | ↓ |
| **Stream Frequency** $F_s$ ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |
| **Slope** $S$ ↑ | ↑ (faster flow) | ↑ (significantly) | ↑↑ (very strong) | ↓ (strongly) |
| **Relief** $H$ ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↓ |
| **Ruggedness** $R_n$ ↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑↑ | ↓ |
| **Soil infiltration capacity** ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ |
| **Surface roughness** (Manning's $n$) ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ |
| **Forest cover** ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓↓ | ↑ |

## 3.5 Quantitative Worked Example: Comparing Two Sub-Watersheds

**Problem:** Two sub-watersheds in the Western Ghats near Wayanad, Kerala, are being evaluated for *erosion risk ranking* under the MGNREGA watershed programme.

| Parameter | Sub-watershed A | Sub-watershed B |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Area $A$ ($\text{km}^2$) | 25 | 25 |
| Perimeter $P$ (km) | 22 | 30 |
| Basin length $L_b$ (km) | 8 | 12 |
| Max elevation (m) | 1450 | 1450 |
| Min elevation (m) | 850 | 850 |
| Total stream length $\sum L_i$ (km) | 75 | 35 |
| No. of streams $N_s$ | 50 | 15 |

**Step 1 — Relief $H$:** Both are the same, $H = 1450 - 850 = 600$ m.

**Step 2 — Drainage density $D_d$:**

$$
D_d^A = \frac{75}{25} = 3.0 \text{ km/km}^2 \quad ; \quad D_d^B = \frac{35}{25} = 1.4 \text{ km/km}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Stream frequency $F_s$:**

$$
F_s^A = \frac{50}{25} = 2.0 / \text{km}^2 \quad ; \quad F_s^B = \frac{15}{25} = 0.6 / \text{km}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Form factor $R_f$:**

$$
R_f^A = \frac{25}{8^2} = 0.391 \quad ; \quad R_f^B = \frac{25}{12^2} = 0.174
$$

**Step 5 — Compactness $C_c$:**

$$
C_c^A = \frac{0.2821 \times 22}{\sqrt{25}} = \frac{6.206}{5} = 1.241
$$

$$
C_c^B = \frac{0.2821 \times 30}{\sqrt{25}} = \frac{8.463}{5} = 1.693
$$

**Step 6 — Ruggedness $R_n$ (using $H$ in km = 0.6):**

$$
R_n^A = 3.0 \times 0.6 = 1.80 \quad ; \quad R_n^B = 1.4 \times 0.6 = 0.84
$$

**Step 7 — Infiltration number $I_f$:**

$$
I_f^A = 3.0 \times 2.0 = 6.0 \quad ; \quad I_f^B = 1.4 \times 0.6 = 0.84
$$

**Ranking Conclusion (Erosion Risk):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sub-watershed A is the HIGHER PRIORITY for soil conservation** because it has:
> - Higher $D_d$ (3.0 vs 1.4) → *more dissected* → *faster runoff concentration*.
> - Higher $F_s$ (2.0 vs 0.6) → *more channels* → *higher sediment transport capacity*.
> - Higher $R_f$ (0.391 vs 0.174) → *more compact* → *sharper, earlier peak*.
> - Higher $R_n$ (1.80 vs 0.84) → *more rugged* → *higher erosion potential*.
> - Higher $I_f$ (6.0 vs 0.84) → *more infiltration-impeding texture*.
> - Lower $C_c$ (1.241 vs 1.693) → *more circular* → *less travel-time smoothing*.
>
> **Conservation interventions prioritised in A:** check-dams, contour bunds, afforestation, terracing — *not in B* (where B can wait or receive lighter treatment).

This is the **practical value** of morphometric analysis in KTU field projects and government watershed programmes.

## 4.3 Process Flow: How a Property Becomes a Management Action

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Measure Dd from DEM] --> P2{Is Dd greater than 3.0?}
    P2 -->|YES| P3[Flag as High Dissection]
    P2 -->|NO| P4[Flag as Low Dissection]
    P3 --> P5[Compute Rn]
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6{Is Rn greater than 1.0?}
    P6 -->|YES| P7[PRIORITY 1: Highly Rugged]
    P6 -->|NO| P8[PRIORITY 3: Low Ruggedness]
    P7 --> P9[Plan Check Dams and Afforestation]
    P8 --> P10[Light Treatment Only]
    P9 --> P11[Annual Monitoring]
    P10 --> P11
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - **Diagram 4.1** shows the *one-to-many* mapping: each physical property group contributes to a single *hydrologic response vector*, which then drives the *management decision matrix*.
> - **Diagram 4.2** shows the *data-to-decision* pipeline used in KTU field projects (toposheets + DEM → GIS extraction → morphometric formulas → priority list).
> - **Diagram 4.3** shows a *decision tree* (a simplified version of what is taught in Watershed Prioritisation labs).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions are direct, definition-based, and require 3-4 sentence precise answers. No sub-parts.

### Q1. Define drainage density and explain its hydrological significance. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Drainage density $D_d$ is defined as the **total length of all streams per unit area of the watershed**:

$$
D_d = \frac{\sum L_i}{A} \quad [\text{km/km}^2]
$$

**Hydrological significance:**
- $D_d$ indicates the *closeness of channel spacing* and hence the *efficiency* of the drainage system.
- **High $D_d$** (e.g., $> 3.0 \text{ km/km}^2$) implies *fine drainage texture*, *faster runoff response*, *shorter time of concentration*, and *higher peak discharge*.
- **Low $D_d$** (e.g., $< 1.0 \text{ km/km}^2$) implies *coarse texture*, *longer overland flow*, *more infiltration*, and *delayed, attenuated runoff*.
- $D_d$ is controlled by *lithology*, *infiltration capacity*, *relief*, and *rainfall intensity*. In Kerala, laterite catchments have $D_d \approx 1.5-3.0$, while crystalline gneiss catchments can exceed $4.0$.

---

### Q2. What is meant by the ruggedness number of a watershed? Mention its significance. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Ruggedness Number** $R_n$ (Strahler, 1958) is a *compound dimensionless index* that combines the *horizontal dissection* of a watershed with its *vertical relief*:

$$
R_n = D_d \times H
$$

where $D_d$ = drainage density ($\text{km/km}^2$) and $H$ = total relief (km).

**Significance:**
- $R_n$ quantifies the *overall topographic ruggedness* and *erosional energy* of a basin.
- A high $R_n$ watershed has both *steep slopes* and *dense channel networks*, leading to *rapid runoff*, *high sediment yield*, and *flash-flood susceptibility*.
- Used in *watershed prioritisation* for conservation, in *landslide susceptibility mapping* (relevant to Western Ghats), and in *erosion modelling* (USLE/RUSLE factor refinement).
- Typical ranges: $R_n < 0.3$ = low ruggedness; $0.3 - 1.0$ = moderate; $R_n > 1.0$ = high (problematic).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has TWO sub-parts (typically 7 + 7 marks). The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B. Model solutions must include diagrams/tables and a *keyword-rich* answer body for valuation.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain in detail the effect of watershed size, shape, and slope on runoff and peak discharge. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Effect of Size (Area, $A$):** (2 marks)

The size of a watershed is the most fundamental geometric property. The general effects are:

- **Peak discharge** $Q_p$ generally *increases* with $A$, but **not linearly** — peak discharge per unit area *decreases* as area increases (because larger basins have more *spatial variability* in rainfall, more *channel storage*, more *floodplain attenuation*, and more *base-flow* contribution).
- The Rational Method $Q_p = C I A$ uses $A$ directly.
- Larger basins produce *flatter, broader* hydrographs; smaller basins produce *sharper, narrower* peaks.
- Time of concentration $T_c$ increases with $A$ (longer flow paths).

**2. Effect of Shape ($R_f$, $R_e$, $R_c$, $C_c$):** (2.5 marks)

The shape determines the *time-spread* of runoff contributions at the outlet. Two watersheds of equal area can produce vastly different hydrographs based on shape:

- A **circular (high $R_f$, high $R_c$, $C_c \to 1$)** watershed delivers water from all points *nearly simultaneously*, producing a **high, sharp, early peak**.
- An **elongated (low $R_f$, low $R_c$, $C_c \gg 1$)** watershed delivers water in *staggered* fashion, producing a **lower, broader, delayed peak**.
- Practical example: A *circular 5 km² basin* in Kerala with $R_f = 0.7$ may have $Q_p = 80 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, while an *elongated 5 km² basin* with $R_f = 0.2$ may have $Q_p = 45 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ for the same storm.

**3. Effect of Slope ($S$, $R_h$, $R_n$):** (2.5 marks)

Slope is the *driving force* for overland and channel flow. Effects:

- Higher slope → *higher flow velocity* (Manning: $v \propto S^{1/2}$).
- Higher slope → *shorter $T_c$* (Kirpich: $T_c \propto S^{-0.385}$).
- Higher slope → *higher $Q_p$* (peak arrives earlier and higher).
- Higher slope → *higher erosion and sediment transport capacity* (shear stress $\tau \propto S$).
- Watershed *average slope* and *channel slope* have different effects: channel slope dominates *transmission losses* and *flood routing*, while overland slope dominates *runoff generation*.

**Conclusion:** The three properties act *jointly*: a *small, circular, steep* watershed in Kerala is the *worst-case flood scenario* (Wayanad 2018, 2019 examples); a *large, elongated, gentle* watershed is the *most flood-mitigated*.

---

#### (b) With neat sketches/tables, describe the various linear, areal, and relief parameters of a watershed. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The morphometric parameters of a watershed are classified into **three groups**:

| Group | Parameter | Formula | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Linear** | Stream order $u$ | Strahler method | Hierarchical rank |
| | Stream length $L$ | — | Length of each stream |
| | Mean stream length $\bar{L}_u$ | $\sum L_i / N_i$ | Average per order |
| | Basin length $L_b$ | Longest flow path | Outlet to divide |
| | Basin perimeter $P$ | — | Length of divide |
| | Bifurcation ratio $R_b$ | $N_u / N_{u+1}$ | 3-5 typically |
| | Length ratio $R_L$ | $\bar{L}_u / \bar{L}_{u-1}$ | Geometric growth |
| **Areal** | Area $A$ | GIS / planimeter | Plan area |
| | Drainage density $D_d$ | $\sum L_i / A$ | Texture of dissection |
| | Stream frequency $F_s$ | $N_s / A$ | Stream count per area |
| | Infiltration number $I_f$ | $D_d \times F_s$ | Combined texture |
| | Form factor $R_f$ | $A / L_b^2$ | Shape index |
| | Elongation ratio $R_e$ | $1.128\sqrt{A} / L_b$ | Shape index |
| | Circularity ratio $R_c$ | $4\pi A / P^2$ | Circularity |
| | Compactness $C_c$ | $0.2821 P / \sqrt{A}$ | Gravelius |
| | Length of overland flow $L_o$ | $1 / (2 D_d)$ | Horton |
| | Constant of channel maintenance $C$ | $1 / D_d$ | Inverse of $D_d$ |
| | Drainage texture $T$ | $N_s / P$ | Smith (1950) |
| **Relief** | Maximum relief $H$ | $H_{max} - H_{min}$ | Elevation drop |
| | Relief ratio $R_h$ | $H / L_b$ | Steepness |
| | Relative relief $R_{rel}$ | $H / P$ | Per perimeter |
| | Ruggedness number $R_n$ | $D_d \times H$ | Strahler |
| | Dissection index $D_i$ | $H / H_{max}$ | — |
| | Hypsometric integral $HI$ | area-elevation curve | Basin maturity |

**Sketch description (to be drawn in exam):** A watershed outline showing:
- (i) **Drainage divide** (dashed line around perimeter $P$).
- (ii) **Outlet / pour point** (lowest point).
- (iii) **Stream network** with Strahler orders (1, 2, 3, ...) marked.
- (iv) **Basin length** $L_b$ drawn as a straight line from outlet to farthest divide point.
- (v) **Highest point** and **lowest point** marked with elevations.
- (vi) **Contour lines** (e.g., 20 m interval) showing slope distribution.
- (vii) A **hypsometric curve** in a side panel (area-elevation distribution).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Do not mix up $D_d$ (km/km²) with stream *frequency* $F_s$ (number/km²).** They are *different* parameters and serve *different* purposes.
> 2. **Units matter:** Always carry $H$ in **km** when computing $R_n$ for a dimensionless answer; report $T_c$ in **minutes** for Kirpich.
> 3. **$R_f$ is dimensionless**; do not write units.
> 4. **Always draw the watershed outline** in the answer script for Part B. Examiners award *sketch marks* (1-2 marks) separately — students who skip sketches lose easy marks.
> 5. **Mention the Strahler (1957) stream ordering convention** explicitly — *first-order streams are the fingertip tributaries*; two first-order streams join to form a *second-order*, etc.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) Discuss the importance of physical properties of a watershed in hydrological analysis. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

The physical (morphometric) properties of a watershed are the *primary determinants* of its hydrological behaviour. Their importance is summarised under five heads:

**1. Predicting Runoff and Peak Flow:** (2 marks)
The size, shape, slope, and drainage density of a watershed directly determine the *magnitude* and *timing* of runoff. Methods like the *Rational Formula* ($Q_p = CIA$) and the *SCS-CN method* use area, slope, and land use as primary inputs. Synthetic unit hydrographs (Clark, Snyder, SCS) use $T_c$ and $L_b$.

**2. Estimating Time of Concentration ($T_c$):** (1 mark)
Kirpich, Kerby, and SCS lag equations all depend on $L$, $S$, and roughness. $T_c$ in turn governs the *design storm duration* and *time-to-peak* of the hydrograph.

**3. Erosion and Sediment Yield Assessment:** (1.5 marks)
High $D_d$, $F_s$, $R_n$, and slope imply *high erosion potential*. The Universal Soil Loss Equation (USLE) embeds slope and slope-length factors that depend directly on basin morphometry. Watershed prioritisation for soil conservation (MGNREGA, IWMP) is *fundamentally* a morphometric exercise.

**4. Flood Management and Reservoir Sizing:** (1.5 marks)
The shape and size of a basin determine the *design flood*; the relief and drainage density determine the *rate of reservoir siltation*. Morphometric inputs feed the *modified Puls* and *Muskingum* routing methods.

**5. Land Use Planning and Disaster Management:** (1 mark)
In Kerala's Western Ghats, morphometric maps are used by KSDMA to identify *landslide-prone* sub-watersheds (high $R_n$ + high $D_d$ + high slope). Watershed-based land-use planning (agroforestry, terracing, afforestation) is *prescribed* based on morphometric ranking.

**Conclusion:** Physical properties are the *connective tissue* between *geography* and *hydrology* — every quantitative watershed analysis begins here.

---

#### (b) Derive the mathematical expressions for drainage density, stream frequency, and form factor. Discuss their relative importance in flood peak estimation. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Drainage Density $D_d$:** (2 marks)

By definition (Horton, 1932, 1945):

$$
D_d = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N_s} L_i}{A}
$$

where $\sum L_i$ = total length of all streams (km) and $A$ = basin area ($\text{km}^2$). Unit: $\text{km/km}^2$.

**Physical meaning:** $D_d$ measures the *total length of stream channel per unit area* — a proxy for the *efficiency* of the drainage system in *removing surface water*.

**2. Stream Frequency $F_s$:** (2 marks)

By definition (Horton, 1932):

$$
F_s = \frac{N_s}{A}
$$

where $N_s$ = total number of streams of *all orders*. Unit: $\text{number/km}^2$.

**Physical meaning:** $F_s$ measures the *number of streams per unit area* — a proxy for the *texture* of the drainage network. It correlates with *lithology* (more permeable rock → fewer streams) and *rainfall intensity*.

**3. Form Factor $R_f$:** (2 marks)

By definition (Horton, 1932):

$$
R_f = \frac{A}{L_b^2}
$$

where $A$ = basin area and $L_b$ = basin length (longest dimension). It is dimensionless.

**Derivation logic:** For a *perfect circle* of area $A$, the longest flow path is the diameter $2\sqrt{A/\pi}$, so:

$$
R_f^{\text{circle}} = \frac{A}{(2\sqrt{A/\pi})^2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.7854
$$

Hence $R_f$ ranges from **0 (highly elongated)** to **0.7854 (circular)**.

**4. Relative Importance in Flood Peak Estimation:** (1 mark)

| Parameter | Direct Effect on $Q_p$ | Indirect Effect | Verdict |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $D_d$ | Strong ↑ ($D_d$ ↑ ⇒ $Q_p$ ↑) | Shorter $T_c$ | **Most influential** |
| $F_s$ | Moderate ↑ | Indicates texture | Supporting role |
| $R_f$ | Strong ↑ (more circular ⇒ higher peak) | Shorter $T_c$ | **Most influential** (shape) |

For design-flood estimation in Kerala, $D_d$ and $R_f$ are the *primary* morphometric predictors; $F_s$ is *corroborative*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorise Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **Watershed** = area enclosed by a drainage divide, draining to a single outlet.
- [ ] **Three groups of physical properties:** Linear, Areal, Relief.
- [ ] **Drainage density** $D_d = \sum L_i / A$ — high $D_d$ = high dissection = *faster* runoff, *shorter* $T_c$, *higher* $Q_p$.
- [ ] **Stream frequency** $F_s = N_s / A$ — high $F_s$ = fine texture = *faster* runoff.
- [ ] **Form factor** $R_f = A / L_b^2$ — range 0 to $\pi/4 \approx 0.7854$. Higher ⇒ *more circular* ⇒ *sharper peak*.
- [ ] **Elongation ratio** $R_e = 1.128\sqrt{A} / L_b$ — range 0 to 1. Higher ⇒ *more circular*.
- [ ] **Circularity ratio** $R_c = 4\pi A / P^2$ — 1 for a perfect circle.
- [ ] **Compactness coefficient** $C_c = 0.2821 P / \sqrt{A}$ — 1 for a perfect circle.
- [ ] **Length of overland flow** $L_o = 1 / (2 D_d)$ (Horton).
- [ ] **Relief ratio** $R_h = H / L_b$ — high $R_h$ = steep, short basin.
- [ ] **Ruggedness number** $R_n = D_d \times H$ — high $R_n$ = rugged, erosion-prone (Western Ghats!).
- [ ] **Kirpich** $T_c = 0.01947 L^{0.77} / S^{0.385}$ — most used formula, $L$ in m, $S$ in m/m, $T_c$ in min.
- [ ] **Strahler (1957) stream ordering:** Two streams of order $u$ join → order $u+1$.
- [ ] **Bifurcation ratio** $R_b = N_u / N_{u+1}$ — typically 3 to 5.
- [ ] **Shape** affects *time-spread* of runoff; **Size** affects *magnitude*; **Slope** affects *velocity & erosion*.
- [ ] **High-priority** sub-watersheds for conservation: high $D_d$, high $F_s$, high $R_n$, high $R_f$, low $C_c$.
- [ ] **Kerala context:** Laterite catchments $D_d \approx 1.5-3.0$; crystalline gneiss can exceed 4.0; coastal alluvial $< 1.0$.
- [ ] **Always** include a watershed outline sketch in 14-mark answers.
- [ ] **Always** specify units; convert $H$ to km for dimensionless $R_n$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance of Watershed Properties: Effect of Geomorphologic Factors and Associated Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET723 - WATERSHED CONSERVATION AND MANGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Importance of Watershed Properties: Effect of Geomorphologic Factors and Associated Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining the Watershed

A **watershed** (also called a drainage basin or catchment) is a geographically delineated area of land where all precipitation that falls upon it drains to a common outlet — a river, lake, reservoir, estuary, or ocean. The boundary separating one watershed from another is termed a **drainage divide** or **water parting line**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Formal):** A watershed is a topographically bounded hydrologic unit, within which the entire hydrologic cycle (precipitation → surface runoff → subsurface flow → evaporation) operates as an integrated system, governed by its geomorphology, geology, soil, vegetation, and land-use characteristics.

## 1.2 Defining Geomorphology

**Geomorphology** is the scientific study of the origin, evolution, and spatial configuration of landforms produced by physical, chemical, and biological processes operating at or near the Earth's surface. When applied to a watershed, it examines the **shape, size, relief, slope, drainage network, and terrain features** that govern how water and sediment move through the system.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Umbrella and the Funnel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition for Students (Plain English):**
> Imagine a giant open **umbrella** held upside down on a hill during heavy rain. The fabric of the umbrella is the **watershed** (it collects every drop). The handle's bottom tip is the **outlet**. The seams running along the fabric are the **streams/tributaries** that channel the water. Now, if the umbrella is:
> - **Steep and round** → water gushes out fast (flashy runoff, high erosion).
> - **Long and narrow (elongated)** → water trickles out slowly (delayed runoff, less peak flood).
> - **Full of holes (high infiltration soil)** → water soaks in (more groundwater, less surface loss).
>
> Geomorphology is essentially the **"shape, slope, and seam-pattern" of the umbrella**, and it decides *how* the rain is delivered to the outlet. This is why studying geomorphology is the first step in any watershed conservation plan.

## 1.4 Why Geomorphology Matters in Watershed Management

The geomorphic character of a watershed is the **master variable** that controls:

- The **quantity** and **timing** of runoff (hydrograph shape).
- The **volume** of **sediment yield** transported to the outlet.
- The **rate of soil erosion** on hill slopes.
- The **groundwater recharge potential**.
- The **susceptibility to floods, landslides, and droughts**.
- The **suitability for structures** such as check dams, percolation tanks, and contour bunds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric:** Watershed areas are commonly measured in **km²**, slopes in **percent (%)** or **degrees (°)**, drainage density in **km/km²**, and stream orders using the **Strahler (1957) classification system** as the KTU 2024 Scheme standard.

## 1.5 Key Geomorphologic Properties of a Watershed

| # | Property | What It Describes |
|---|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Area (A)** | Total planimetric extent of the basin |
| 2 | **Shape** (form factor, circularity, elongation) | Planform geometry |
| 3 | **Slope / Relief** | Vertical drop per unit horizontal distance |
| 4 | **Drainage Network** | Pattern, density, and stream order |
| 5 | **Aspect** | Compass direction a slope faces |
| 6 | **Lithology & Structure** | Bedrock type, joints, faults, folds |
| 7 | **Soil mantle** | Depth, texture, permeability |
| 8 | **Land use / Cover** | Vegetation, built-up, bare areas |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Linear and Areal Properties

### (A) Watershed Area (A)
The total surface projected onto a horizontal plane that contributes runoff to the outlet. It is the most fundamental property because almost every other hydrologic/geomorphic index scales with A.

### (B) Watershed Length (L_b)
The straight-line distance from the outlet to the **farthest point on the watershed boundary**, measured parallel to the principal drainage line.

### (C) Perimeter (P)
The total length of the drainage divide that encloses the basin.

## 2.2 Shape Indices (Shape = How the Basin Stores vs. Releases Water)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Shape Matters:** A circular basin delivers runoff simultaneously from all points → **high peak flow, short time of concentration**. An elongated basin delivers runoff in stages → **low peak flow, long time of concentration**. KTU questions on this distinction appear almost every year.

### (a) Form Factor (F_f) — Horton (1932)

$$F_f = \dfrac{A}{L_b^{\,2}}$$

- Range: **0 (highly elongated) → 1 (perfectly circular)**
- F_f = **0.4 to 0.8** is typical for natural basins; lower values indicate elongated basins with flatter hydrographs.

### (b) Circularity Ratio (R_c) — Miller (1953)

$$R_c = \dfrac{4\pi A}{P^{\,2}}$$

- Compares basin area to the area of a circle having the same perimeter.
- R_c → 1 indicates a near-circular basin; lower values suggest an elongated or irregular basin.

### (c) Elongation Ratio (R_e) — Schumm (1956)

$$R_e = \dfrac{1.128 \sqrt{A}}{L_b}$$

- Range: **0 (line) → 1 (circle)**
- R_e values:
  - 0.9 – 0.10 → very elongated
  - 0.8 – 0.9 → elongated
  - 0.7 – 0.8 → less elongated
  - < 0.7 → circular

## 2.3 Relief Properties

### (a) Relief (R)
$$R = H_{\max} - H_{\min}$$

where H_max is the highest elevation on the basin boundary and H_min is the elevation at the outlet.

### (b) Relief Ratio (R_r) — Schumm (1956)

$$R_r = \dfrac{R}{L_b}$$

- Steep basins have **high R_r** and produce **high sediment yield**.

### (c) Relative Relief (R_p) — Melton (1957)

$$R_p = \dfrac{R}{P} \times 100 \,\%$$

### (d) Ruggedness Number (R_n) — Melton (1957)

$$R_n = R \times D_d$$

where D_d is drainage density (km/km²). High ruggedness → high erosion potential.

### (e) Hypsometric Integral (HI) — Strahler (1952)

$$HI = \dfrac{H_{\text{mean}} - H_{\min}}{H_{\max} - H_{\min}}$$

- HI > 0.6 → youthfully dissected (high erosion, steep slopes dominant).
- HI 0.3 – 0.6 → mature / equilibrium (moderate erosion).
- HI < 0.3 → old / monadnock stage (low erosion, gentle slopes).

## 2.4 Drainage Network Properties

### (a) Stream Order (u) — Strahler System

- **1st order**: rills and gullies with no tributaries.
- **2nd order**: formed by confluence of two 1st-order streams.
- **3rd order**: formed by confluence of two 2nd-order streams, and so on.
- The watershed's principal stream has the **highest order** (e.g., a 4th-order basin).

### (b) Horton's Laws of Drainage Composition

**Law I — Bifurcation Ratio (R_b):**

$$R_b = \dfrac{N_u}{N_{u+1}}$$

- N_u = number of streams of order u.
- For natural basins, R_b usually lies between **3 and 5** (typical ≈ 4).

**Law II — Length Ratio (R_L):**

$$R_L = \dfrac{\overline{L}_{u+1}}{\overline{L}_u}$$

- L_u = mean length of streams of order u.

### (c) Drainage Density (D_d) — Horton (1945)

$$D_d = \dfrac{\Sigma L_i}{A}$$

- Σ L_i = total length of all streams (km); A = basin area (km²).
- Units: **km / km²**
- Interpretation:
  - D_d < 2 → low density (pervious, vegetated, low runoff).
  - D_d = 2 to 5 → moderate.
  - D_d > 5 → high density (impervious / resistant rock, high runoff, high erosion).

### (d) Stream Frequency (F_s) — Horton

$$F_s = \dfrac{\Sigma N_u}{A}$$

Units: **number / km²**

### (e) Drainage Texture (T) — Horton

$$T = \dfrac{\Sigma N_u}{P}$$

### (f) Infiltration Number (I_n)

$$I_n = D_d \times F_s$$

### (g) Constant of Channel Maintenance (C_m) — Schumm

$$C_m = \dfrac{1}{D_d}$$

- Area needed to sustain 1 km of stream channel.

## 2.5 Slope Properties

**Average Slope (S) of the watershed** is calculated by:

$$S = \dfrac{N \cdot \Sigma L_x}{\Sigma A_x}$$

- N = number of contour intervals; L_x = total length of contour x; A_x = area between two successive contours.

Or by the **Wentworth method**:

$$S = \dfrac{H \times L_{\text{contour}}}{A}$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **high-yield** for KTU ESE and internal examinations. Memorize symbols and units.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Form Factor | F_f = A / L_b² | 0 – 1 |
| 2 | Circularity Ratio | R_c = 4πA / P² | 0 – 1 |
| 3 | Elongation Ratio | R_e = 1.128√A / L_b | 0 – 1 |
| 4 | Relief Ratio | R_r = R / L_b | dimensionless |
| 5 | Relative Relief | R_p = (R/P) × 100% | % |
| 6 | Ruggedness Number | R_n = R × D_d | – |
| 7 | Hypsometric Integral | HI = (H_mean − H_min) / (H_max − H_min) | 0 – 1 |
| 8 | Bifurcation Ratio | R_b = N_u / N_{u+1} | 3 – 5 |
| 9 | Length Ratio | R_L = L̄_{u+1} / L̄_u | 1.5 – 3.5 |
| 10 | Drainage Density | D_d = ΣL_i / A | km/km² |
| 11 | Stream Frequency | F_s = ΣN_u / A | km⁻² |
| 12 | Drainage Texture | T = ΣN_u / P | km⁻¹ |
| 13 | Infiltration Number | I_n = D_d × F_s | km⁻² |
| 14 | Maintenance Constant | C_m = 1 / D_d | km²/km |
| 15 | Average Slope | S = N·ΣL_x / ΣA_x | m/m or % |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Flood estimation** (Rational Method, SCN, unit hydrograph) uses F_f, R_e, D_d, S, and L_b.
- **Reservoir sedimentation studies** use R_r, R_n, and HI to predict long-term silt inflow.
- **Landslide susceptibility mapping** uses slope, aspect, lithology, and drainage density.
- **Watershed prioritization** for soil conservation uses a Compound Parameter = (R_r × R_n × D_d × F_s).
- **Site selection for check dams and percolation tanks** uses D_d, F_s, and slope to ensure geological feasibility and effective storage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bifurcation Ratio as an Indicator of Basin Shape

The bifurcation ratio R_b is one of the most diagnostic geomorphic indices. Its **theoretical** minimum is **2** (perfectly symmetric, dendritic network) and its **practical maximum** is **5** (highly asymmetric, tectonically disturbed network).

**Step 1 — Definition of R_b**

By Horton's (1945) First Law of Drainage Composition, the number of streams decreases in a geometric progression as stream order increases. Hence:

$$
\frac{N_u}{N_{u+1}} = R_b = \text{constant for a given basin}
$$

**Step 2 — Geometric series of stream counts**

Integrating over all orders from 1 to n:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_1 &= R_b^{\,n-1} \cdot N_n \\
N_1 + N_2 + \cdots + N_n &= N_1 \left( 1 + \frac{1}{R_b} + \frac{1}{R_b^2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{R_b^{n-1}} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum of geometric progression**

The bracketed term is a finite geometric series. With ratio 1/R_b:

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \left( \frac{1}{R_b} \right)^{k} = \frac{1 - (1/R_b)^{n}}{1 - (1/R_b)} = \frac{R_b (1 - R_b^{-n})}{R_b - 1}
$$

**Step 4 — Total number of streams**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma N_u &= N_1 \cdot \frac{R_b (1 - R_b^{-n})}{R_b - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation**

For a 4th-order basin with N₁ = 64 streams, n = 4, R_b = 4:

$$
N_4 = \frac{64}{4^{4-1}} = \frac{64}{64} = 1
$$

This confirms Horton's law and validates the dendritic model. **High R_b (>5)** indicates geological structural control (faults, joints); **low R_b (<3)** suggests uniform lithology and homogeneous terrain.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Horton's Law of Stream Lengths

**Step 1 — Empirical observation**

Horton observed that the mean length of streams of a given order is a constant multiple of the mean length of streams of the next lower order:

$$
\overline{L}_{u+1} = R_L \cdot \overline{L}_u
$$

**Step 2 — Geometric expansion**

Iterating from order 1 to order n:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{L}_n &= R_L^{\,n-1} \cdot \overline{L}_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total stream length**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma L_i = \sum_{u=1}^{n} N_u \cdot \overline{L}_u &= \sum_{u=1}^{n} \left( N_1 R_b^{-(u-1)} \right) \left( \overline{L}_1 R_L^{\,u-1} \right) \\
&= N_1 \overline{L}_1 \sum_{u=1}^{n} \left( \frac{R_L}{R_b} \right)^{u-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitution into D_d**

$$
D_d = \frac{\Sigma L_i}{A} = \frac{N_1 \overline{L}_1}{A} \cdot \frac{1 - (R_L / R_b)^n}{1 - (R_L / R_b)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** Since R_L < R_b in almost all natural basins, the geometric term converges. This convergence is what makes D_d a **stable, scale-invariant** descriptor of basin texture.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Hypsometric Integral from Contour Data

**Step 1 — Tabulate contour data**

Let H_1 > H_2 > … > H_n be the contour elevations, and A_1 < A_2 < … < A_n be the cumulative area above each contour.

**Step 2 — Trapezoidal area under the hypsometric curve**

The hypsometric curve plots **relative area (a = A_x / A)** versus **relative elevation (h = (H_x − H_min) / (H_max − H_min))**.

**Step 3 — Compute HI as the area under this curve**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HI &= \int_{0}^{1} a(h) \, dh \\
   &\approx \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} \frac{(a_k + a_{k+1})}{2} \cdot (h_{k+1} - h_k)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplified form**

For a 3-contour basin:

$$
HI \approx \frac{a_1 + a_2}{2} \cdot (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{a_2 + a_3}{2} \cdot (h_3 - h_2)
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example**

Given:
- H_max = 1000 m, H_min = 200 m, H_mean = 700 m
- Then: HI = (700 − 200) / (1000 − 200) = 500/800 = **0.625**

This indicates a **youthful basin** with substantial erosion potential and steep, undissected terrain still present.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Slope by the Wentworth Method

**Step 1 — Identify all contour intervals (N)** within the basin.

**Step 2 — For each contour interval x, measure:**
- L_x = total length of the contour (km)
- A_x = area between contour x and the next higher contour (km²)

**Step 3 — Compute contour interval in elevation:**

$$
I_x = H_{x} - H_{x+1}
$$

**Step 4 — Mean slope of interval x:**

$$
S_x = \frac{I_x \cdot L_x}{A_x}
$$

**Step 5 — Watershed-average slope:**

$$
S = \frac{\Sigma S_x \cdot A_x}{\Sigma A_x} = \frac{\Sigma (I_x \cdot L_x)}{\Sigma A_x}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the standard method used by the **All India Soil and Land Use Survey (AISLUS)** and the **Central Water Commission (CWC)** for KTU-affiliated project work.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Each Geomorphologic Factor

| Geomorphologic Factor | Physical Process Influenced | Effect on Runoff | Effect on Sediment Yield | Effect on Infiltration | Watershed Management Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **High Slope (S ↑)** | Overland flow velocity ↑ | Peak discharge ↑ | Erosion ↑ (sheet, rill) | ↓ | Terracing, benching, contour bunds required |
| **Steep Relief (R_r ↑)** | Gravitational energy of flow ↑ | Hydrograph flashiness ↑ | Mass wasting ↑, landslides | ↓ | Afforestation, retaining walls |
| **Circular Shape (F_f ↑)** | Synchronous arrival at outlet | Peak flow ↑, T_c ↓ | ↑ | ↓ | Flood-control structures, detention basins |
| **Elongated Shape (F_f ↓)** | Staggered arrival at outlet | Peak flow ↓, T_c ↑ | ↓ | – | Lower flood risk, more uniform base flow |
| **High D_d (D_d ↑)** | Dense drainage network | Quick runoff, ↑ peak | ↑ | ↓ (impervious subsoil) | Conservation structures on every order-1 channel |
| **Low D_d (D_d ↓)** | Sparse channels | Slow runoff, ↑ lag time | ↓ | ↑ (pervious rock) | Recharge structures, percolation tanks |
| **High F_s** | Many 1st-order rills | ↑ | ↑ ↑ (rill + gully erosion) | ↓ | Gully plugging, brushwood check dams |
| **Aspect — South/West in N. Hemisphere** | Solar radiation ↑, evapotranspiration ↑ | ↓ | ↑ (drier soil) | ↓ | Drought-resistant vegetation preferred |
| **Aspect — North/East** | Cooler, moister microclimate | ↑? | ↓ | ↑ | Natural forest retention recommended |
| **Lithology — Igneous/Resistant** | High runoff coefficient | ↑ | ↓ (less weatherable) | ↓ | Limited recharge; runoff harvesting |
| **Lithology — Sedimentary/Permeable** | High infiltration | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ | Recharge-friendly; tanks and tubewells |
| **Tectonically Disturbed (R_b > 5)** | Structural control of channels | Variable | Variable (often ↑) | Variable | Geological site-investigation mandatory |
| **Hypsometric Integral > 0.6** | Youthful terrain | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | Priority for soil-conservation works |
| **High Ruggedness Number (R_n)** | Combined effect of R and D_d | ↑ | ↑ ↑ | ↓ | High priority for afforestation and terracing |

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How Geomorphology Drives the Watershed System

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **cause-and-effect logic** of how a watershed's geomorphic properties cascade into hydrologic response, sediment movement, and management decisions. Physical drawings of a real terrain are not used; instead, the flow captures all functional interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    D0 --> D1[Time of Concentration Tc]
    D0 --> D2[Peak Discharge Qp]
    D0 --> D3[Runoff Volume V]
    D0 --> D4[Baseflow and Recharge]

    E0 --> E1[Sheet Erosion]
    E0 --> E2[Rill and Gully Erosion]
    E0 --> E3[Mass Wasting: Landslide, Slump]
    E0 --> E4[Sediment Yield at Outlet]

    F0 --> F1[Soil Conservation: Terracing, Bunding]

    F0 --> F3[Recharge: Percolation Pond, Recharge Shaft]
    F0 --> F4[Flood Mitigation: Detention Basin, Levee]

    C1 --> D1

    C2 --> D1

    C2 --> E1

    C2 --> E3
    C3 --> D1

    D2 --> F4

    F1 --> G0[STABLE WATERSHED]

    F3 --> G0

```

## 4.2 Functional Reading of the Diagram

1. **Rainfall Input** is the driving force.
2. **Geomorphic Framework** is the *control panel* — it does not produce water but determines **how, where, and how fast** the rainfall is partitioned into runoff, infiltration, evaporation, and sediment.
3. The **terrain morphometry** block feeds into the **hydrologic** and **sediment** responses.
4. Each failure mode (high erosion, low recharge, flood risk) maps onto a **specific management tool** in the response block.
5. All interventions converge to the goal: a **stable, productive, and resilient watershed**.

## 4.3 Decision Tree for Watershed Prioritization (KTU Project Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Drainage Density Dd] --> A1{Dd greater than 5}
    A1 -- YES --> A2[High Priority]
    A1 -- NO --> A3{Dd between 2 and 5}
    A3 -- YES --> A4[Moderate Priority]
    A3 -- NO --> A5[Low Priority]
    B0[Form Factor Ff] --> B1{Ff greater than 0.7}
    B1 -- YES --> B2[Flash Flood Risk]
    B1 -- NO --> B3[Lower Peak Risk]
    C0[Ruggedness Rn] --> C1{Rn greater than 0.4}
    C1 -- YES --> C2[High Erosion Risk]
    C1 -- NO --> C3[Low Erosion Risk]
    A2 --> Z[Compound Priority Index]
    A4 --> Z
    A5 --> Z
    B2 --> Z
    B3 --> Z
    C2 --> Z
    C3 --> Z
    Z --> OUT[Ranked List for Soil Conservation Works]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme Mini-Project, students are required to compute at least **D_d, F_s, F_f, R_e, R_r, and R_n** for their study watershed, then derive a Compound Parameter (CP) for prioritization using the formula:
>
> $$CP = R_r \times R_n \times D_d \times F_s$$
>
> Higher CP = Higher priority for conservation works.
