| **Synoptic View** | A single satellite scene covers thousands of km², ideal for medium/large watersheds (e.g., Landsat-8 covers 185 × 185 km) |
| **Multi-temporal Coverage** | Repeat-pass imaging enables change detection (e.g., deforestation, reservoir siltation) |
| **Multi-spectral Data** | Multiple bands allow derivation of vegetation, water, and soil indices |
| **Inaccessible Terrain** | Reaches remote, rugged, or conflict-affected watersheds safely |
| **Quantitative & Repeatable** | Provides objective, georeferenced data for hydrological modelling (SWAT, HEC-HMS) |
| **Cost-Effective** | Free data from Landsat, Sentinel, MODIS reduce field survey expenses |
| **Integration with GIS** | Direct compatibility with DEM generation, slope, flow accumulation, and watershed delineation |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Compilation — Remote Sensing Indices Used in Watershed Analysis**

| # | Index / Parameter | Formula | Application in Watershed Analysis | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **NDVI** (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index) | $\text{NDVI} = \dfrac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}}$ | Vegetation vigour, crop health, erosion-prone bare areas | $-1$ to $+1$ |
| 2 | **NDWI** (Normalized Difference Water Index) | $\text{NDWI} = \dfrac{\text{Green} - \text{NIR}}{\text{Green} + \text{NIR}}$ (McFeeters, 1996) | Open water body delineation, surface water extent mapping | $-1$ to $+1$ |
| 3 | **MNDWI** (Modified NDWI) | $\text{MNDWI} = \dfrac{\text{Green} - \text{SWIR}}{\text{Green} + \text{SWIR}}$ (Xu, 2006) | Improved water body extraction in urban/built-up watersheds | $-1$ to $+1$ |
| 4 | **NDMI** (Normalized Difference Moisture Index) | $\text{NDMI} = \dfrac{\text{NIR} - \text{SWIR}}{\text{NIR} + \text{SWIR}}$ | Canopy/soil moisture, drought monitoring | $-1$ to $+1$ |
| 5 | **SAVI** (Soil-Adjusted Vegetation Index) | $\text{SAVI} = \dfrac{(\text{NIR} - \text{Red})}{(\text{NIR} + \text{Red} + L)} \cdot (1 + L)$, with $L = 0.5$ | Reduces soil background effect in sparse vegetation watersheds | $-1$ to $+1$ |
| 6 | **NDBI** (Normalized Difference Built-up Index) | $\text{NDBI} = \dfrac{\text{SWIR} - \text{NIR}}{\text{SWIR} + \text{NIR}}$ | Maps urban sprawl in watersheds | $-1$ to $+1$ |
| 7 | **EVI** (Enhanced Vegetation Index) | $\text{EVI} = 2.5 \cdot \dfrac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + 6\text{Red} - 7.5\text{Blue} + 1}$ | Reduces atmospheric and soil noise; dense vegetation | $-1$ to $+1$ |
| 8 | **Band Ratio Water Index (BRWI)** | $\text{BRWI} = \dfrac{\text{Green}}{\text{NIR}}$ | Simple water detection | $\geq 1$ for water |
| 9 | **Tasseled Cap Wetness** | Linear combination of 6 bands | Soil and vegetation moisture content | Continuous |
| 10 | **Radiometric Resolution** | $L = 2^n$ discrete levels, $n$ = bits | $8$-bit = $2^8 = 256$ DN levels | — |
| 11 | **Areal Scale** | $A = N \cdot (R)^2$, $N$ = pixels, $R$ = spatial resolution (m) | Estimate watershed area from image | km² |
| 12 | **Drainage Density** | $D_d = \dfrac{\Sigma L_i}{A}$, $L_i$ = stream length, $A$ = area | From RS-derived stream network | km⁻¹ |
| 13 | **Stream Frequency** | $F_s = \dfrac{N_s}{A}$, $N_s$ = number of streams | From RS-classified channels | km⁻² |
| 14 | **Bifurcation Ratio** | $R_b = \dfrac{N_u}{N_{u+1}}$ | Morphometric indicator of watershed shape | Dimensionless |
| 15 | **Form Factor** | $R_f = \dfrac{A}{L_b^2}$, $L_b$ = basin length | Shape indicator — circular vs. elongated | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Table Symbols:** All absolute value, modulus, and "such that" notations have been written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ to preserve Markdown table integrity. The KTU examiner will be very strict about NDVI sign conventions: **negative NDVI → water/cloud; zero → bare soil; positive → vegetation**.

### 2.4 Spectral Signatures of Watershed Features

Different watershed targets have unique spectral reflectance curves:

- **Clear Water:** Very low reflectance in NIR (~0%), peak in blue-green (~0.4 µm).
- **Turbid Water:** Higher reflectance in green and red due to suspended sediments.
- **Healthy Vegetation:** Low Red, high NIR → high NDVI.
- **Bare Soil:** Moderate, gradually increasing reflectance from VIS to NIR.
- **Built-up/Asphalt:** Low VIS, moderate NIR, high SWIR.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Spectral signatures form the **physical basis of image classification** (supervised, unsupervised, object-based). Watershed managers classify the LULC map to compute runoff coefficients, sediment yield, and to identify erosion-prone zones for conservation planning.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Physical Basis of the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI)

The NDVI is a dimensionless index designed to exploit the contrasting reflectance of vegetation in the Red and NIR bands. The derivation follows directly from the spectral signature of chlorophyll.

**Step 1 — Define Spectral Reflectance**

Let $\rho_R$ be the reflectance in the Red band (e.g., Band 4 of Landsat-8, centred at **0.655 µm**) and $\rho_{NIR}$ be the reflectance in the Near-Infrared band (Band 5 of Landsat-8, centred at **0.865 µm**).

**Step 2 — Express Chlorophyll Absorption**

Chlorophyll-$\alpha$ and Chlorophyll-$\beta$ pigments in healthy leaves absorb strongly in the Red region for photosynthesis. Hence, for healthy vegetation:

$$\rho_R \ll \rho_{NIR}$$

**Step 3 — Express Mesophyll Scattering**

In the NIR region, the spongy mesophyll cells of the leaf scatter radiation strongly. Hence, for healthy vegetation:

$$\rho_{NIR} \gg \rho_R$$

**Step 4 — Construct the Normalized Ratio**

The **Simple Ratio (SR)** is defined as:

$$\text{SR} = \frac{\rho_{NIR}}{\rho_R}$$

For dense vegetation, $\text{SR}$ is large, but the value is unbounded and not standardized.

**Step 5 — Normalize to a Fixed Range [−1, +1]**

To obtain a bounded, comparable index, the difference is normalized by the sum:

$$\text{NDVI} = \frac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_R}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_R}$$

**Step 6 — Verify the Range**

- If $\rho_{NIR} = \rho_R$ (bare soil, no chlorophyll effect), then $\text{NDVI} = 0$.
- If $\rho_{NIR} \gg \rho_R$ (dense vegetation), $\text{NDVI} \to +1$.
- If $\rho_{NIR} \ll \rho_R$ (water body), $\text{NDVI} \to -1$.

This matches the spectral behaviour described earlier. The index is therefore a robust proxy for **vegetation cover and vigour**, with watershed applications including crop stress detection, deforestation monitoring, and erosion-risk mapping.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — NDWI and MNDWI for Water Body Delineation

**Step 1 — Physical Reasoning**

Water strongly absorbs NIR and SWIR radiation, while it reflects green light moderately. Built-up land, in contrast, reflects strongly in SWIR (concrete, dry soil) but less in NIR. This contrast allows water to be separated from built-up land.

**Step 2 — NDWI (McFeeters, 1996)**

$$\text{NDWI} = \frac{\rho_{\text{Green}} - \rho_{NIR}}{\rho_{\text{Green}} + \rho_{NIR}}$$

- For water: $\rho_{\text{Green}}$ is moderate, $\rho_{NIR}$ is very low → $\text{NDWI} > 0$.
- For vegetation: $\rho_{\text{Green}}$ is low, $\rho_{NIR}$ is very high → $\text{NDWI} < 0$.
- For bare soil: $\text{NDWI} \approx 0$.

**Step 3 — Modified NDWI (Xu, 2006)**

In urban or mixed watersheds, NDWI may misclassify built-up land as water because built-up surfaces also have low NIR. Xu replaced NIR with SWIR to enhance water contrast:

$$\text{MNDWI} = \frac{\rho_{\text{Green}} - \rho_{SWIR}}{\rho_{\text{Green}} + \rho_{SWIR}}$$

- For built-up: $\rho_{SWIR}$ is high → $\text{MNDWI} < 0$.
- For water: $\rho_{SWIR}$ is very low (strong absorption) → $\text{MNDWI} > 0$.

**Step 4 — Thresholding for Binary Water Mask**

A typical threshold is $\text{MNDWI} > 0$ for water, but this is calibrated using ground truth and histogram analysis. The water mask is then used to compute:

$$A_{\text{water}} = N_{\text{water}} \cdot (R_{\text{pixel}})^2$$

where $N_{\text{water}}$ is the number of water pixels and $R_{\text{pixel}}$ is the spatial resolution in metres.

**Step 5 — Numerical Example**

Suppose a Sentinel-2 scene (10 m resolution) has $4{,}500$ water pixels in a watershed.
- $R_{\text{pixel}} = 10$ m, so each pixel area = $100$ m².
- Total water area: $4{,}500 \times 100 = 4.5 \times 10^5$ m² $= 0.45$ km².
- If watershed area is 15 km², **water spread index** = $0.45 / 15 = 3\%$, useful for monitoring reservoir shrinkage, lake desiccation, or flood inundation.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Drainage Density from RS-Derived Stream Network

**Step 1 — Source Data**

Stream networks are extracted from:
- **Optical imagery** (visual interpretation of drainage),
- **SAR/shadow imagery** (water appears dark),
- **DEM-derived flow accumulation** (SRTM, ALOS-PALSAR, Cartosat DEM).

**Step 2 — Definition**

Drainage density $D_d$ is the total length of streams per unit area of the watershed:

$$D_d = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N_s} L_i}{A}$$

where $L_i$ is the length of the $i$-th stream segment (km) and $A$ is the watershed area (km²).

**Step 3 — Numerical Worked Example**

Consider a watershed with the following observed stream segments from a digital line network extracted from a Cartosat-1 DEM:

| Stream Order | Number of Streams $N_u$ | Mean Length (km) |
|---|---|---|
| 1st | 25 | 0.8 |
| 2nd | 9 | 1.4 |
| 3rd | 3 | 2.6 |
| 4th | 1 | 4.0 |
| **Total** | **38** | — |

Total stream length: $\Sigma L_i = (25 \times 0.8) + (9 \times 1.4) + (3 \times 2.6) + (1 \times 4.0) = 20.0 + 12.6 + 7.8 + 4.0 = 44.4$ km.

If watershed area $A = 30$ km², then:

$$D_d = \frac{44.4 \text{ km}}{30 \text{ km}^2} = 1.48 \text{ km}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation**

- $D_d < 1$ km⁻¹ → coarse drainage, permeable subsoil, low runoff potential.
- $1 < D_d < 2$ km⁻¹ → moderate drainage.
- $D_d > 2$ km⁻¹ → fine drainage, impermeable terrain, high erosion risk.

Thus, $D_d = 1.48$ km⁻¹ indicates a moderately drained watershed, requiring soil and water conservation measures such as **contour bunding, terracing, and check dams**.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Bifurcation Ratio and Horton's Law of Stream Numbers

**Step 1 — Horton's Law of Stream Numbers**

Horton (1945) postulated that the number of streams in successive orders follows a geometric series:

$$N_u = R_b^{(Ω - u)}$$

where $N_u$ is the number of streams of order $u$, $Ω$ is the highest order, and $R_b$ is the bifurcation ratio.

**Step 2 — Solve for the Bifurcation Ratio**

$$R_b = \frac{N_u}{N_{u+1}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute from the Data Above**

$$R_{b,1-2} = \frac{25}{9} = 2.78, \quad R_{b,2-3} = \frac{9}{3} = 3.00, \quad R_{b,3-4} = \frac{3}{1} = 3.00$$

Average bifurcation ratio:

$$\overline{R_b} = \frac{2.78 + 3.00 + 3.00}{3} = 2.93$$

**Step 4 — Geological Interpretation**

- $R_b = 2$ to $4$ → typical of natural watersheds with uniform geology.
- $R_b > 5$ → indicates strong structural or lithological control.
- $\overline{R_b} = 2.93$ → **geologically homogeneous watershed**, suggesting uniform runoff response and moderate flood peaks.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping RS Outputs to Watershed Parameters

| RS Output (Data Product) | Derived Watershed Parameter | Engineering Decision |
|---|---|---|
| **LULC map** (supervised classification) | Runoff coefficient $C$, Curve Number $CN$ | SCS-CN runoff estimation |
| **NDVI raster** | Vegetation density, crop coefficient $K_c$ | FAO-56 ET₀ estimation |
| **NDWI/MNDWI raster** | Surface water area, reservoir storage | Flood/drought monitoring |
| **DEM (SRTM, ALOS, Cartosat)** | Slope, aspect, flow direction, watershed boundary | SWAT, HEC-HMS, AnnAGNPS models |
| **Soil line from spectral reflectance** | Soil organic matter, parent material | USLE erodibility factor $K$ |
| **SAR backscatter $\sigma^0$** | Soil moisture, roughness | Inundation mapping |
| **Thermal IR (LST)** | Land Surface Temperature, soil heat flux | SEBAL/METRIC for actual ET |
| **Change detection (multi-date)** | Deforestation rate, urban expansion | Conservation prioritization |
| **Shoreline digitization** | Reservoir siltation rate, lake shrinkage | Catchment treatment plans |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Remote Sensing Workflow in Watershed Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Energy Source Sun or Active Transmitter] --> B[Atmospheric Interaction Scattering and Absorption]
    B --> C[Watershed Surface Interaction with EMR]
    C --> D[Sensor Platform Satellite Aircraft UAV]
    D --> E[Sensor Detector Multispectral Hyperspectral SAR]
    E --> F[Raw Digital Number DN Image]
    F --> G[Preprocessing Radiometric Geometric Atmospheric Correction]
    G --> H[Image Enhancement Contrast Stretch Filtering]
    H --> I[Feature Extraction Indices NDVI NDWI MNDWI NDMI]
    I --> J[Classification Supervised Unsupervised Object Based]
    J --> K[Watershed Geo Products LULC Map DEM Water Bodies Vegetation Cover]
    K --> L[GIS Integration Overlay Analysis Morphometry]
    L --> M[Hydrological Modeling SWAT HEC HMS SCS CN]
    M --> N[Engineering Decisions Conservation Planning Check Dams Terracing Flood Control]
```

**Figure 4.1 — End-to-End Remote Sensing Workflow for Watershed Analysis**
The diagram traces the **Energy → Atmosphere → Target → Sensor → Processing → Product → Decision** chain. Each block represents a stage in which watershed-relevant information is extracted or refined.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Role of Multi-Sensor Data in Watershed Characterization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OpticalData[Optical Sensors]
        A1[Landsat 8 OLI 30 m] --> B1[LULC NDVI]
        A2[Sentinel 2 MSI 10 to 20 m] --> B1
        A3[Resourcesat LISS III 23.5 m] --> B1
    end
    subgraph ThermalData[Thermal Sensors]
        C1[Landsat 8 TIRS 100 m] --> D1[LST Evapotranspiration]
        C2[MODIS 1 km] --> D1
    end
    subgraph MicrowaveData[Microwave Sensors]
        E1[Sentinel 1 SAR 10 to 20 m] --> F1[Soil Moisture Flood Mapping]
        E2[ALOS PALSAR 25 m] --> F1
    end
    subgraph ElevationData[Elevation Data]
        G1[SRTM 30 m] --> H1[Slope Aspect Watershed Delineation]
        G2[ALOS PALSAR DEM 12.5 m] --> H1
        G3[Cartosat DEM 10 m] --> H1
        G4[LiDAR 1 to 5 m] --> H1
    end
    B1 --> I1[Integrated Watershed GIS Database]
    D1 --> I1
    F1 --> I1
    H1 --> I1
    I1 --> J1[Watershed Management Plan]
```

**Figure 4.2 — Multi-Sensor Contribution to Watershed Database**
This block diagram demonstrates that **no single sensor provides all watershed variables**. Optical data contributes LULC; thermal data yields LST/ET; microwave data penetrates clouds for flood mapping; and elevation data forms the morphometric backbone. The integrated database drives the **Watershed Management Plan**.

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Spectral Index Decision Tree for Watershed Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Multispectral Image] --> B{Identify Target Feature}
    B --> C1[Water Body]
    B --> C2[Vegetation]
    B --> C3[Built up Land]
    B --> C4[Bare Soil]
    B --> C5[Soil Moisture]
    C1 --> D1[Apply MNDWI Green and SWIR]
    C2 --> D2[Apply NDVI NIR and Red]
    C3 --> D3[Apply NDBI SWIR and NIR]
    C4 --> D4[Apply SAVI NIR and Red with L factor]
    C5 --> D5[Apply NDMI NIR and SWIR]
    D1 --> E1[Threshold greater than zero for water]
    D2 --> E2[Threshold greater than 0.2 for vegetation]
    D3 --> E3[Threshold greater than zero for built up]
    D4 --> E4[Threshold zero to 0.3 for bare soil]
    D5 --> E5[Threshold positive for moisture]
    E1 --> F[Output Classified Watershed Map]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
```

**Figure 4.3 — Spectral Index Decision Tree for Watershed Feature Mapping**
This decision-tree flowchart is a **block-level functional architecture** mapping how different spectral indices are routed based on the target feature of interest in a watershed.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** Define Remote Sensing. Distinguish between Passive and Active Remote Sensing with two examples each.

**Model Answer:**
Remote Sensing is the science and technology of obtaining information about objects or areas from a distance, typically from sensors on platforms such as satellites or aircraft, by detecting electromagnetic radiation reflected or emitted from the Earth's surface. In watershed analysis, it is used to derive LULC, vegetation indices, water spread, and terrain information.

| Aspect | Passive RS | Active RS |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | Natural (Sun, thermal emission) | Sensor-emitted (RADAR, LiDAR) |
| **Day/Night Operation** | Daytime (for solar) | Day and night |
| **Weather** | Affected by clouds | Penetrates clouds (microwave) |
| **Example Sensors** | Landsat-8 OLI, Sentinel-2 MSI, MODIS | Sentinel-1 SAR, ALOS-PALSAR, LiDAR |
| **Watershed Use** | LULC, NDVI, water delineation | Flood mapping, soil moisture, DEM |

**[3 Marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark distinction table + 1 mark examples]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** What is NDVI? Explain its physical basis and significance in watershed management.

**Model Answer:**
The Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) is computed as:

$$\text{NDVI} = \frac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{Red}}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{Red}}$$

**Physical Basis:** Healthy chlorophyll in vegetation absorbs Red light strongly (for photosynthesis) and the spongy mesophyll scatters Near-Infrared (NIR) strongly. This contrast makes NDVI a robust vegetation proxy.

**Significance in Watershed Management:**
- Quantifies vegetation cover for **erosion-risk mapping** (low NDVI = high erosion risk).
- Inputs to **crop coefficient (Kc)** for evapotranspiration estimation.
- Monitors **deforestation and land degradation** through change detection.
- Provides a **C-factor** for the Universal Soil Loss Equation (USLE).

Range: −1 (water/cloud) to +1 (dense vegetation). Values > 0.2 generally indicate vegetation presence.

**[3 Marks: 1 mark formula + 1 mark physical basis + 1 mark watershed significance]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option A**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the different stages of remote sensing data acquisition and processing, with reference to their application in watershed analysis. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Describe the role of NDWI and MNDWI in water body delineation. Apply the MNDWI formula to a hypothetical watershed to compute the water spread index. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The remote sensing process consists of **seven interlinked stages**:

1. **Energy Source:** The Sun provides EMR (passive) or the sensor itself emits a pulse (active). For watershed studies, solar illumination in the VIS-NIR-SWIR range is the most common.
2. **Atmosphere Interaction:** Atmospheric scattering (Rayleigh, Mie) and absorption (by ozone, water vapour, CO₂) modify the signal. Atmospheric correction (e.g., FLAASH, Sen2Cor, Dark Object Subtraction) is mandatory for accurate reflectance retrieval in watershed classification.
3. **Target Interaction:** The watershed surface (water, vegetation, soil, urban) absorbs, transmits, and reflects EMR according to its physical and chemical properties. The spectral signature is the fingerprint used in classification.
4. **Sensor Platform:** Sensors are mounted on satellites (e.g., Landsat-8, Sentinel-2), aircraft, or UAVs. Choice depends on spatial, spectral, temporal, and radiometric resolution requirements.
5. **Sensor Detection:** The detector records the incoming photons and converts them to an electrical signal, then to a Digital Number (DN). Each DN is stored in a pixel grid.
6. **Data Transmission & Pre-processing:** Raw data is telemetered to ground stations. Pre-processing includes:
   - *Radiometric correction* (converting DN to radiance/reflectance),
   - *Geometric correction* (orthorectification, geo-referencing to UTM/WGS84),
   - *Atmospheric correction* (removing haze and path radiance),
   - *Noise removal* (destriping, dead pixel correction).
7. **Information Extraction & Integration:** Indices (NDVI, NDWI), classification (supervised/unsupervised/OBIA), and DEM generation produce watershed products (LULC, water map, slope). Integration with GIS enables hydrological modelling (SWAT, HEC-HMS), morphometric analysis, and conservation planning.

**Watershed application example:** A complete Landsat-8 scene is atmospherically corrected → classified into 5 LULC classes (forest, agriculture, water, built-up, fallow) → fed into SCS-CN model to estimate runoff → used for prioritization of micro-watersheds for treatment.

**[7 Marks breakdown: 5 stages × 1 mark each + 2 marks for watershed application]**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

The **Normalized Difference Water Index (NDWI)** by McFeeters (1996) and its **Modified version (MNDWI)** by Xu (2006) are spectral indices used to delineate water bodies from optical satellite imagery.

**NDWI Formula:**

$$\text{NDWI} = \frac{\rho_{Green} - \rho_{NIR}}{\rho_{Green} + \rho_{NIR}}$$

**MNDWI Formula (improves water extraction in urban areas):**

$$\text{MNDWI} = \frac{\rho_{Green} - \rho_{SWIR}}{\rho_{Green} + \rho_{SWIR}}$$

**Physical Basis:** Water strongly absorbs NIR and SWIR but reflects green light. Built-up land also has low NIR but high SWIR — hence MNDWI replaces NIR with SWIR to enhance the contrast between water and built-up surfaces.

**Watershed Applications:**
- Reservoir and lake boundary extraction.
- Flood inundation mapping.
- Monitoring seasonal water spread variation.
- Estimating reservoir capacity loss due to siltation.
- Drought assessment (water body shrinkage).

**Hypothetical Numerical Application:**

Consider a Sentinel-2 image (10 m spatial resolution) of a watershed. The MNDWI raster is generated and thresholded at MNDWI > 0 to extract water pixels. Suppose:
- Number of water pixels, $N_{water} = 12{,}500$
- Spatial resolution, $R = 10$ m
- Total watershed area, $A_{ws} = 25$ km² = $25 \times 10^6$ m²

**Step 1 — Compute Water Area:**

Each pixel area = $10 \times 10 = 100$ m².

$$A_{water} = N_{water} \times R^2 = 12{,}500 \times 100 = 1.25 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2 = 1.25 \text{ km}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute Water Spread Index (WSI):**

$$\text{WSI} = \frac{A_{water}}{A_{ws}} \times 100\% = \frac{1.25}{25} \times 100\% = 5\%$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Interpretation:**

A 5% water spread is moderate. For a 25 km² watershed in Kerala, this could correspond to a small reservoir or wetland. The WSI can be compared across multiple dates to assess **flood inundation extent** (monsoon vs. pre-monsoon) or **drought-induced shrinkage** for adaptive watershed management.

**[7 Marks breakdown: 2 marks for formulas + 2 marks for physical basis and applications + 3 marks for numerical computation]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option B**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Style]**
**(a)** Discuss the various satellite platforms and sensors used for watershed characterization. Compare the spatial, spectral, and temporal resolutions of Landsat-8, Sentinel-2, and Sentinel-1. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Describe how a Digital Elevation Model (DEM) derived from remote sensing is used for morphometric analysis of a watershed. Compute drainage density and bifurcation ratio for the given data. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Major Satellite Platforms for Watershed Characterization:**

| Sensor | Type | Spatial Resolution | Spectral Bands | Temporal Resolution | Watershed Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Landsat-8 OLI/TIRS** | Optical + Thermal | 30 m (VIS-SWIR), 100 m (TIR) | 11 bands | 16 days | LULC, NDVI, LST, water |
| **Sentinel-2 MSI** | Optical | 10–20 m | 13 bands | 5 days | LULC, NDVI, NDWI, agriculture |
| **Sentinel-1 SAR** | Microwave (C-band) | 10–20 m | C-band VV+VH | 6–12 days | Flood, soil moisture |
| **MODIS (Terra/Aqua)** | Optical + Thermal | 250 m – 1 km | 36 bands | 1–2 days | Regional LULC, ET, vegetation |
| **Resourcesat LISS-III** | Optical | 23.5 m | 4 bands | 24 days | LULC, agriculture |
| **ALOS-PALSAR** | Microwave (L-band) | 25 m | L-band HH/HV | 46 days | DEM, forest, flood |
| **Cartosat-1** | Panchromatic | 2.5 m | 1 band | 5 days | High-resolution mapping, DEM |
| **SRTM** | Radar altimetry | 30 m (global) | C-band | Single pass (Feb 2000) | DEM, terrain analysis |
| **LiDAR** | Active laser | 1–5 m | Multiple returns | On-demand | High-resolution DEM, canopy |

**Resolution Comparison:**

| Parameter | Landsat-8 | Sentinel-2 | Sentinel-1 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Spatial** | 30 m | 10–20 m | 10–20 m |
| **Spectral** | 11 bands | 13 bands | 2 polarizations |
| **Radiometric** | 12-bit | 12-bit | Looks (intensity) |
| **Temporal** | 16 days | 5 days | 6–12 days |
| **All-weather** | No (cloud-affected) | No (cloud-affected) | Yes (cloud-penetrating) |

**Trade-off Principle:** Higher spatial resolution = lower swath width and longer revisit period. The choice of sensor depends on **watershed size, project objective, and budget**.

**[7 Marks breakdown: 4 marks for the table + 2 marks for comparison + 1 mark for trade-off]**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

A **Digital Elevation Model (DEM)** is a raster representation of terrain elevation. RS-derived DEMs (SRTM, ALOS-PALSAR, Cartosat, LiDAR) are used to extract morphometric parameters for watershed analysis.

**DEM-Based Morphometric Workflow:**

1. **Fill sinks** in the DEM.
2. Compute **Flow Direction** (D8 algorithm: each cell drains to its steepest neighbour).
3. Compute **Flow Accumulation** (number of upstream cells draining to each cell).
4. Define **stream threshold** (e.g., cells with accumulation > 1000) → derive **stream network**.
5. Delineate **watershed boundary** using pour point and flow direction.
6. Compute **slope, aspect, curvature, hillshade**.
7. Extract **stream order** (Strahler, Shreve).
8. Compute **morphometric parameters** (drainage density, stream frequency, bifurcation ratio, form factor, elongation ratio, etc.).

**Numerical Application:**

Given a watershed of area $A = 30$ km² with the following stream data:

| Stream Order $u$ | Number of Streams $N_u$ | Mean Length (km) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 30 | 0.7 |
| 2 | 12 | 1.2 |
| 3 | 4 | 2.0 |
| 4 | 1 | 3.5 |

**Step 1 — Drainage Density:**

Total length $\Sigma L_i = (30 \times 0.7) + (12 \times 1.2) + (4 \times 2.0) + (1 \times 3.5)$
$= 21.0 + 14.4 + 8.0 + 3.5 = 46.9$ km

$$D_d = \frac{\Sigma L_i}{A} = \frac{46.9}{30} = 1.563 \text{ km}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Bifurcation Ratios:**

$$R_{b,1-2} = \frac{30}{12} = 2.50, \quad R_{b,2-3} = \frac{12}{4} = 3.00, \quad R_{b,3-4} = \frac{4}{1} = 4.00$$

Average:

$$\overline{R_b} = \frac{2.50 + 3.00 + 4.00}{3} = 3.17$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

- $D_d = 1.56$ km⁻¹ → moderately drained, moderate surface runoff, suitable for **terracing and check dams**.
- $\overline{R_b} = 3.17$ → within the natural range (2–4) → **geologically homogeneous** watershed, predictable hydrological response.
- Slight increase in $R_b$ from order 3 to 4 (4.00) may indicate local structural influence.

**[7 Marks breakdown: 3 marks for workflow + 2 marks for $D_d$ + 2 marks for $R_b$ and interpretation]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in RS + Watershed Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing NDWI and MNDWI:** Many students write NDWI formula but apply it to built-up watersheds where MNDWI is required. Always state the McFeeters (1996) and Xu (2006) citations.
> 2. **Wrong sign in NDVI:** Students often forget that **water has negative NDVI**, not zero. A cell with NDVI = 0 is bare soil, not water.
> 3. **Mixing spatial and spectral resolution:** Spatial resolution is in **metres**, spectral resolution is in **number of bands**. Do not interchange.
> 4. **Forgetting atmospheric correction:** Any LULC or index computation should mention **atmospheric correction (FLAASH, Sen2Cor, Dark Object Subtraction)**. Examiners deduct marks for skipping this step.
> 5. **Units of drainage density:** $D_d$ is in **km⁻¹** (length/area). Students often write it incorrectly as km or km².
> 6. **Confusing bifurcation ratio with drainage density:** $R_b$ is a **dimensionless ratio of stream counts**; $D_d$ is a **length per unit area**. They are not interchangeable.
> 7. **Passive vs Active confusion:** RADAR is **active**, not passive. LiDAR is **active**. Sentinel-1 is **active (SAR)**. Sentinel-2 is **passive (optical)**.
> 8. **Skipping the cite/reference:** Naming the scientist (McFeeters, Xu, Horton, Strahler) earns bonus marks.
> 9. **Writing NDVI value beyond [−1, +1]:** Index range is bounded. Values > 1 indicate a miscalculation.
> 10. **Not validating with ground truth:** KTU examiners expect at least a mention of **GPS-based field verification** or **ground truth** for any RS-based classification result.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Applications of Remote Sensing in Watershed Analysis**

- ✅ **Remote Sensing = Non-contact acquisition of information** about a watershed from a distance using EMR.
- ✅ **Passive RS** uses natural energy (Sun); **Active RS** uses sensor-emitted energy (RADAR, LiDAR).
- ✅ **EMR chain:** Source → Atmosphere → Target → Sensor → Data → Information → Decision.
- ✅ **Energy conservation:** $A + T + R = 1$ (for opaque media, $T = 0$).
- ✅ **NDVI** = (NIR − Red) / (NIR + Red) → vegetation; range [−1, +1].
- ✅ **NDWI** = (Green − NIR) / (Green + NIR) → water (McFeeters, 1996).
- ✅ **MNDWI** = (Green − SWIR) / (Green + SWIR) → water in urban watersheds (Xu, 2006).
- ✅ **NDMI** = (NIR − SWIR) / (NIR + SWIR) → moisture/drought.
- ✅ **SAVI** includes soil-line correction factor $L$ (typically $L = 0.5$).
- ✅ **EVI** = $2.5 \cdot (\text{NIR} - \text{Red}) / (\text{NIR} + 6\text{Red} - 7.5\text{Blue} + 1)$ → reduces atmospheric and soil noise.
- ✅ **NDBI** = (SWIR − NIR) / (SWIR + NIR) → built-up areas.
- ✅ **Drainage Density** $D_d = \Sigma L_i / A$ in **km⁻¹** → <1 coarse, 1–2 moderate, >2 fine (high erosion).
- ✅ **Bifurcation Ratio** $R_b = N_u / N_{u+1}$ → range 2–4 natural; >5 structural control.
- ✅ **Form Factor** $R_f = A / L_b^2$ → lower values = more elongated watershed = lower peak flow.
- ✅ **Stream Frequency** $F_s = N_s / A$ → indicates drainage texture.
- ✅ **Four Resolutions:** Spatial (m), Spectral (# bands), Radiometric (bits), Temporal (days).
- ✅ **8-bit image** has $2^8 = 256$ DN levels; **12-bit** has $4{,}096$ levels.
- ✅ **SRTM** (Shuttle Radar Topography Mission, 2000): free global DEM at **30 m / 90 m**.
- ✅ **Sentinel-2** has 5-day revisit and 10–20 m spatial resolution → ideal for Indian watersheds.
- ✅ **Landsat-8 OLI** has 9 spectral bands + 1 panchromatic, 12-bit radiometric resolution.
- ✅ **Pre-processing is mandatory:** Radiometric → Geometric → Atmospheric correction.
- ✅ **Classification types:** Supervised (with training data), Unsupervised (clustering), Object-Based (OBIA).
- ✅ **Accuracy assessment:** Kappa coefficient ≥ 0.8 is desirable for publication quality.
- ✅ **Integration with GIS:** RS outputs feed into SWAT, HEC-HMS, SCS-CN, and USLE models.
- ✅ **Ground truth:** Always validate RS-derived maps with field GPS surveys.
- ✅ **Key scientists to remember:** McFeeters (1996), Xu (2006), Horton (1945), Strahler (1957), Chow (1964).
- ✅ **Spectral signature of water:** Strong absorption in NIR and SWIR; reflects green.
- ✅ **Spectral signature of vegetation:** Low Red, high NIR (red edge at ~0.7 µm).
- ✅ **Watershed applications:** LULC mapping, NDVI, NDWI, DEM, morphometry, soil moisture, flood mapping, reservoir siltation, deforestation, urban sprawl, ET estimation, snow/glacier monitoring (in Himalayan watersheds).
- ✅ **KTU favourite exam topics:** NDVI formula & range, NDWI vs MNDWI, drainage density, bifurcation ratio, passive vs active sensors, Landsat vs Sentinel comparison, role of DEM in watershed delineation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Topographic or Contour Map
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET723 - WATERSHED CONSERVATION AND MANGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Concept of Topographic or Contour Map

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Topographic Map** (also called a **Contour Map**) is a detailed, two-dimensional graphical representation of the three-dimensional features of a land surface. It depicts the natural and man-made features of a region, including relief (elevations and landforms), drainage networks, vegetation, and cultural features (roads, settlements), using standardized symbols, colors, and line conventions prescribed by national surveying agencies (e.g., Survey of India — SOI).

The fundamental building block of a topographic map is the **Contour Line**, which is formally defined as:

> An **imaginary line** drawn on the ground joining points of **equal elevation (equal Reduced Level – R.L.)** above a chosen **datum** (usually Mean Sea Level – MSL).

Every topographic map is therefore a **family of nested, non-intersecting contour lines** projected onto a horizontal plane, with the map scale, contour interval, and grid system providing the geometric and quantitative framework for interpretation.

### Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| Contour Line | Line joining points of equal elevation above MSL. |
| Contour Interval (C.I.) | The vertical distance between two successive contour lines. |
| Horizontal Equivalent (H.E.) | The horizontal distance between two successive contours on the ground. |
| Datum | The reference plane from which all elevations are measured (usually MSL). |
| Reduced Level (R.L.) | The elevation of a point with respect to the chosen datum. |
| Bench Mark (B.M.) | A permanent, surveyed point of known R.L. used as a reference. |
| Scale | The ratio of map distance to actual ground distance. |
| Relief | The variation in elevation of a terrain surface. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** A topographic map is the **primary baseline dataset** for watershed conservation. Without it, no hydrological modeling, slope analysis, drainage delineation, or erosion estimation is possible. Mastery of contour concepts is therefore **non-negotiable** for watershed engineers.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cake-Cutting" Mental Model

Imagine a **3D hill made of sponge cake** sitting on a flat table. Now imagine slicing that cake horizontally with a very sharp knife into many thin, parallel layers — each exactly 1 cm thick. If you place each slice on a piece of paper and trace its outline, you get a series of concentric, closed curves.

**The cake is the terrain. The slices are the contour lines. The paper drawings are the topographic map.**

Just as each cake-slice boundary represents a single elevation (say, all points that are 5 cm above the table, then 6 cm, then 7 cm…), a **contour line represents all points on the ground that share the same elevation**. When you stack all the traced slices back together mentally, you reconstruct the hill. The topographic map does the same — the **closer the contour lines are spaced, the steeper the hill**; the **farther apart, the gentler the slope**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** On a perfectly flat plain, you would get just one giant outline (a single contour encircling the whole area). On a vertical cliff, all the contour lines would coincide into a single line. Topographic maps beautifully encode terrain steepness through contour spacing alone.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following standardized values are used universally in topographic mapping (Survey of India conventions):

- **Standard Contour Intervals:**
  - **Plain / flat terrain** → 0.5 m to 1.0 m
  - **Rolling / hilly terrain** → 2.0 m to 5.0 m
  - **Mountainous terrain** → 10.0 m to 20.0 m
  - **Steep / highly dissected terrain** → 25.0 m to 50.0 m
- **Standard Map Scales (SOI):** 1:25,000, 1:50,000, 1:250,000
- **Datum:** Mean Sea Level (MSL) at Karachi (Survey of India standard) — but in practice, MSL determined at Cochin / Mumbai tide gauges is used for Indian projects.
- **Vertical Datum Offsets:** Typically expressed in **meters (m)** for engineering projects.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Concept — How a Contour Map is "Read"

Reading a topographic map is essentially the inverse operation of viewing the landscape. The map reader interprets line geometry, density, and curvature to reconstruct 3D terrain mentally. The core logic proceeds in four structured steps:

### Step 1 — Identify the Datum and Bench Marks
- The map will state a **datum (e.g., "All elevations referred to MSL")** and locate **Bench Marks (B.M.)** with their R.L. values (e.g., "B.M. 152.345 m" inside a small triangle).
- Every contour elevation is **derived relative to this datum**.

### Step 2 — Recognize Contour Line Types
- **Index Contours:** Every 5th contour is thickened and labeled with its elevation. They serve as reading guides.
- **Intermediate Contours:** The thin lines between index contours. They are the workhorse contours.
- **Supplementary Contours:** Dashed lines showing half-interval elevations in flat terrain (sometimes added to add detail).

### Step 3 — Interpret Spacing, Density, and Shape
- **Closely spaced contours** = **steep slope** (e.g., cliff faces).
- **Widely spaced contours** = **gentle slope** (e.g., plains, valleys).
- **Equally spaced contours** = **uniform slope**.
- **Concentric closed contours** with **decreasing elevation inward** = **hill / mound**.
- **Concentric closed contours** with **increasing elevation inward** = **depression / basin** (often marked with hachures pointing inward).
- **V-shaped contours** with the **V pointing upstream** = **valley / stream**.
- **V-shaped contours** with the **V pointing downhill** = **ridge / spur**.

### Step 4 — Quantitative Analysis
- The map reader can now compute **slopes, areas of sub-catchments, volumes of reservoirs, drainage density**, and so on — entirely from contour geometry.

---

## 2.2 Contour Interval vs. Horizontal Equivalent — The Critical Distinction

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Mathematical Form | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Contour Interval | C.I. (or $h$) | Vertical distance between two successive contours. | Fixed for a given map. | meters (m) |
| Horizontal Equivalent | H.E. (or $d$) | Horizontal ground distance between two successive contours. | Variable, depends on local slope. | meters (m) |
| Slope | $S$ | Ratio of vertical drop to horizontal distance. | $S = \dfrac{\text{C.I.}}{\text{H.E.}} = \dfrac{h}{d}$ | dimensionless or m/m |
| Slope (in %) | $S_\%$ | Slope expressed as percentage. | $S_\% = S \times 100$ | percent (%) |
| Slope (in degrees) | $\theta$ | Slope expressed as an angle. | $\theta = \tan^{-1}(S)$ | degrees (°) |

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Formula | Description | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h = \text{R.L.}_{\text{upper}} - \text{R.L.}_{\text{lower}}$ | Contour interval from labeled contours. | Reading the map's vertical scale. |
| 2 | $S = \dfrac{h}{d}$ | Slope between two adjacent contours. | Slope classification for agriculture, construction. |
| 3 | $S_\% = \dfrac{h}{d} \times 100$ | Percentage slope. | Used in erosion, runoff, and ICAR soil-loss equations. |
| 4 | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{h}{d}\right)$ | Slope angle. | Stability analysis, terrace design. |
| 5 | $A = \sum a_i$ | Catchment area by planimeter / counting squares. | Runoff computation (Rational Method). |
| 6 | $V = \dfrac{h}{3}\,\bigl(A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 A_2}\bigr)$ | Volume between two contours (Prismoidal). | Reservoir capacity, earthwork estimation. |
| 7 | $V = \dfrac{h}{2}\,(A_1 + A_2)$ | Volume between two contours (Trapezoidal / Average End Area). | Quick earthwork estimation. |
| 8 | $R.L._{x} = R.L._{A} + \dfrac{d_{Ax}}{d_{AB}}\,(R.L._{B} - R.L._{A})$ | Interpolation of R.L. of a point $X$ between $A$ and $B$. | Estimating elevation of any point on the map. |
| 9 | $H.E. = \dfrac{\text{C.I.}}{S}$ | Horizontal equivalent for a given slope. | Mapping steep terrain. |
| 10 | $D_d = \dfrac{\sum L_i}{A}$ | Drainage density: total stream length / catchment area. | Watershed hydrology, erosion potential. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in Watershed Conservation:**
> - **Slope $S$** is the single most important input in the **Universal Soil Loss Equation (USLE)** — used to predict soil erosion in a watershed.
> - **Area $A$** and **Drainage Density $D_d$** determine **peak runoff rates** for designing check dams, percolation tanks, and contour bunds.
> - **Contour interval $h$** governs the **precision** of volume estimates for farm ponds and small reservoirs — a critical KTU design problem.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of Topographic Maps

Topographic maps form the **spine of every watershed management project** because they enable:

1. **Drainage Basin Delineation** — Drawing watershed boundaries from ridgelines visible as contour crests.
2. **Slope & Erosion Mapping** — Overlaying slope classes on a GIS layer to identify erosion-prone zones.
3. **Reservoir & Tank Site Selection** — Using contour geometry to identify suitable narrow-valley dam sites and compute storage volumes.
4. **Contour Bunding & Terracing Design** — Marking level lines for soil and water conservation structures.
5. **Floodline & Inundation Mapping** — Predicting areas submerged under different return-period floods.
6. **Land Use Planning** — Zonation of agricultural, forestry, and settlement use based on slope and relief.
7. **Road / Canal Alignment** — Selecting routes with permissible gradients.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation — Interpolation of Elevation of a Point Between Two Contours

### Problem Statement
A point $X$ lies between two contour lines on a topographic map. Contour $A$ has elevation $H_A$ and contour $B$ has elevation $H_B$, with $H_B > H_A$. The perpendicular distance from $X$ to contour $A$ (measured along the line of steepest descent from $B$ to $A$) is $d_{AX}$, and the perpendicular distance between the two contours along that same line is $d_{AB}$. Determine the elevation $H_X$ at point $X$.

### Derivation

**Assumption:** The slope between contours $A$ and $B$ is uniform (locally linear).

By the principle of uniform slope, the rise in elevation is directly proportional to the horizontal distance traversed along the line of steepest slope.

$$
\frac{H_X - H_A}{H_B - H_A} = \frac{d_{AX}}{d_{AB}}
$$

Solving for $H_X$:

$$
H_X - H_A = \left(\frac{d_{AX}}{d_{AB}}\right) \times (H_B - H_A)
$$

$$
H_X = H_A + \left(\frac{d_{AX}}{d_{AB}}\right) \times (H_B - H_A)
$$

This is the **standard linear-interpolation formula** used for reading elevations of off-contour points on a topographic map.

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Given:**
- Contour $A$ has R.L. $= 100$ m
- Contour $B$ has R.L. $= 110$ m
- Distance $d_{AB} = 20$ mm on the map
- Point $X$ is located 8 mm from $A$ along the slope line
- Map scale: 1 cm = 50 m (i.e., 1:5000)

**Step 1 — Convert map distances to ground distances:**
Map scale is 1:5000, so 1 mm on the map = 5000 mm on the ground = 5 m.

$$
d_{AB}(\text{ground}) = 20 \text{ mm} \times 5 \text{ m/mm} = 100 \text{ m}
$$

$$
d_{AX}(\text{ground}) = 8 \text{ mm} \times 5 \text{ m/mm} = 40 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the interpolation formula:**

$$
H_X = H_A + \left(\frac{d_{AX}}{d_{AB}}\right) \times (H_B - H_A)
$$

$$
H_X = 100 + \left(\frac{40}{100}\right) \times (110 - 100)
$$

$$
H_X = 100 + (0.40) \times (10)
$$

$$
H_X = 100 + 4 = 104 \text{ m}
$$

**Result:** The R.L. of point $X$ is **104 m**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A very common mistake students make is to **use map distances directly in the formula without converting to ground distances**. Always convert map mm to ground m using the scale before substitution.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Volume of Reservoir Between Two Contours (Prismoidal / Simpson's Formula)

### Problem Statement
A reservoir is bounded by successive contour lines at elevations $H_1$ and $H_2$, with the contour interval $h = H_2 - H_1$. The plan area enclosed by the lower contour is $A_1$, and the plan area enclosed by the upper contour is $A_2$. Derive an expression for the volume $V$ of the earth (or water) between the two contours.

### Derivation (Prismoidal Approach)

Consider the terrain between the two contours as a **truncated prism** (a frustum-like solid). The cross-sectional area varies smoothly from $A_1$ at the lower elevation to $A_2$ at the upper elevation.

Assuming the cross-sectional area varies as a **second-degree function of elevation** (parabolic profile), apply **Prismoidal Formula**:

$$
V = \frac{h}{3}\,\bigl(A_1 + A_2 + 4 A_m\bigr)
$$

where $A_m$ is the area at the mid-elevation $(H_1 + H_2)/2$.

If we assume $A_m \approx \dfrac{A_1 + A_2}{2}$ (a reasonable linear interpolation of area), then:

$$
V = \frac{h}{3}\,\left(A_1 + A_2 + 4 \cdot \frac{A_1 + A_2}{2}\right)
$$

$$
V = \frac{h}{3}\,\left(A_1 + A_2 + 2(A_1 + A_2)\right)
$$

$$
V = \frac{h}{3}\,\left(3(A_1 + A_2)\right)
$$

$$
V = h\,(A_1 + A_2)
$$

Wait — that is the trapezoidal result. Let us retain the **exact Simpson's rule** for two successive intervals (3 contours), which is the most common KTU case:

For a series of three contours at elevations $H_1$, $H_2$, $H_3$ with equal interval $h$ and areas $A_1$, $A_2$, $A_3$:

$$
V = \frac{h}{3}\,\bigl(A_1 + 4 A_2 + A_3\bigr)
$$

For just **two contours** (one interval), the simpler **Trapezoidal Rule** is the more commonly used and accepted form in KTU solutions:

$$
V = \frac{h}{2}\,(A_1 + A_2)
$$

### Numerical Worked Example

**Given:**
- Contour at 100 m encloses area $A_1 = 20{,}000 \text{ m}^2$
- Contour at 110 m encloses area $A_2 = 8{,}000 \text{ m}^2$
- Contour interval $h = 10$ m

**Apply Trapezoidal Rule:**

$$
V = \frac{h}{2}\,(A_1 + A_2) = \frac{10}{2}\,(20{,}000 + 8{,}000)
$$

$$
V = 5 \times 28{,}000 = 140{,}000 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Result:** The volume of water stored between the 100 m and 110 m contours is **140,000 m³ (1.4 lakh m³)**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Slope Calculation Methods

| Method | Input | Formula | Best Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct (H.E. method)** | Two adjacent contours. | $S = \dfrac{\text{C.I.}}{\text{H.E.}}$ | Field checks, simple slope classes. |
| **Map distance + Scale** | Two points A and B with R.L.s. | $S = \dfrac{\Delta H}{M \cdot d_{map}}$ | Spot slope along a line. |
| **Percentage slope** | Slope value $S$. | $S_\% = S \times 100$ | Erosion, ICAR, terrace spacing norms. |
| **Angle of slope** | Slope value $S$. | $\theta = \tan^{-1}(S)$ | Stability, cut-slope design. |
| **Average slope of watershed** | Contour lengths $L_i$ and C.I. | $\bar{S} = \dfrac{\text{C.I.} \cdot \sum L_i}{A}$ | SCS-CN, USLE applications. |

### Derivation — Average Slope of a Watershed (Contour-Length Method)

A watershed is bounded by a series of contour lines. The total relief drop is approximately C.I. The total "horizontal descent" through the watershed is the sum of the lengths of all contour lines that fall **inside** the watershed. Therefore:

$$
\bar{S} = \frac{\text{C.I.} \times \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i}{A}
$$

where $L_i$ is the length of the $i$-th contour (in m) within the watershed, and $A$ is the plan area of the watershed (in m²). This is a **direct area-weighted average**, and is the standard method taught in KTU watershed courses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to compute the "average slope of a watershed from a contour map," always state the contour-length method explicitly, list the contour lengths (you may be given a table), and apply the formula above. Marks are awarded for the **method statement**, not just the final number.

---

## 3.4 Properties of Contour Lines (Comprehensive List for KTU)

A student should commit the following **properties** to memory, as KTU examiners test them directly in Part A questions:

1. Contour lines **never cross** each other (except in the rare case of an overhanging cliff, where the crossing contour is marked with a special symbol).
2. Contour lines **never split or branch** (except in the case of a vertical cliff where multiple contours coincide).
3. The **spacing** of contour lines is **inversely proportional** to the slope.
4. Closed contours with **decreasing elevation inward** represent a **hill**; with **increasing elevation inward**, a **depression**.
5. Contour lines cross a **valley / stream** at right angles, with the **V pointing upstream** (towards higher elevation).
6. Contour lines cross a **ridge / spur** with the **V pointing downhill** (towards lower elevation).
7. Contours form a **complete loop** around a hill or depression, never disappearing at the map boundary unless the feature continues beyond.
8. All points on the **same contour** have the **same elevation**.
9. **Index contours** (every 5th) are drawn thicker and labeled with their elevation.
10. The **minimum contour interval** for a given map scale is fixed by the legibility of the printed line (typically 0.25 mm on paper).

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Topographic Map

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the **hierarchical components** of a topographic map and how they interact to convey terrain information.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Topographic Map] --> A1[Map Frame and Grid]
    A0 --> A2[Contour Lines]
    A0 --> A3[Hydrographic Features]
    A0 --> A4[Cultural Features]
    A0 --> A5[Cartographic Text]

    A1 --> B1[Latitude and Longitude]
    A1 --> B2[UTM Grid]
    A1 --> B3[Sheet Number and Scale]

    A2 --> C1[Index Contours Thick Labelled]
    A2 --> C2[Intermediate Contours Thin]
    A2 --> C3[Supplementary Contours Dashed]

    A3 --> D1[Streams and Rivers]
    A3 --> D2[Lakes and Ponds]
    A3 --> D3[Watershed Boundary]

    A4 --> E1[Roads and Railways]
    A4 --> E2[Settlements and Buildings]
    A4 --> E3[Land Use Patterns]

    A5 --> F1[Legend and Symbols]
    A5 --> F2[Bench Mark and Spot Heights]
    A5 --> F3[Sheet Title and Datum]

    C1 --> G1[Enable Slope Calculation]
    C2 --> G1
    C3 --> G1
    D3 --> G2[Enable Watershed Delineation]
    D1 --> G2
    C1 --> G2
    B2 --> G3[Enable Area and Distance Measurement]
    B3 --> G3
    F2 --> G4[Enable Absolute Elevation Reference]
    F1 --> G5[Enable Feature Interpretation]
```

---

## 4.2 Decision Logic — Interpreting Contour Patterns

The following Mermaid decision-tree helps a student **decode any terrain feature** from its contour pattern — a high-yield skill for KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0{Contour Pattern} --> S1{Is it a closed loop?}
    S1 -- Yes --> S2{Elevation increases inward?}
    S1 -- No --> S3{Does the contour bend into a V shape?}
    S2 -- Yes --> S4[Depressed Basin or Sink]
    S2 -- No --> S5[Hill or Mound]
    S3 -- Yes --> S6{V points to higher elevation?}
    S3 -- No --> S7{Spacing pattern}
    S6 -- Yes --> S8[Valley or Stream Channel]
    S6 -- No --> S9[Ridge or Spur]
    S7 --> S10{Contours are closely spaced}
    S7 --> S11{Contours are widely spaced}
    S10 --> S12[Steep Slope or Cliff]
    S11 --> S13[Gentle Slope or Plain]
```

---

## 4.3 Workflow — Watershed Delineation from a Topographic Map

The following block diagram summarizes the **operational sequence** for delineating a watershed from a topographic map — a key watershed-management skill.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W1[Identify Outlet Point] --> W2[Trace Perpendiculars to Contours Upstream]
    W2 --> W3[Connect Highest Points along Ridgelines]
    W3 --> W4[Close the Boundary along Ridgeline]
    W4 --> W5[Watershed Boundary Established]
    W5 --> W6[Measure Plan Area A]
    W5 --> W7[Measure Stream Length L]
    W6 --> W8[Compute Drainage Density Dd = L by A]
    W7 --> W8
    W6 --> W9[Compute Average Slope from Contour Lengths]
    W8 --> W10[Feed into Hydrological Model]
    W9 --> W10
```
